finding_ranks.summary: Any local process can gain fully authenticated operator-level access by connecting to the loopback WebSocket endpoint without providing any credentials, because the `trusted-proxy` branch unconditionally returns `ok: true` for all loopback requests before validating proxy headers or a secret token.

When an AbortSignal fires during a foreground exec call, resolving with a normal `status: "failed"` result instead of rejecting bypasses the abort-preservation logic, causing canceled runs to be treated as ordinary tool completions and incorrectly surfacing results back into the agent loop after the turn should have stopped.

The refactored code removes the `applyAuthorizationHeaderForUrl` and `safeFetchWithPolicy` guards that previously stripped `Authorization` headers on redirects to non-allowlisted hosts, risking Graph bearer token leakage to arbitrary redirected destinations.

Runtime log files containing sensitive local credentials, file paths, and a Telegram chat ID were accidentally committed to the repository, exposing private environment details to all repo readers and tarball consumers.

The removal of the `clearUnboundScopes()` guard allows device-less operator sessions authenticated via shared token/password or trusted-proxy Control UI to retain self-declared scopes (e.g., `operator.admin`) that should be device-bound, enabling unauthorized privilege escalation.

The `runTestCase` function never actually invokes the skill or model, instead hardcoding `actual` from the prompt input, causing `skills test`/`bench` commands to evaluate a fabricated response rather than real skill output—making test results meaningless.

Removing the `secrets` CI job eliminates all enforcement of private key detection, workflow security auditing, and production dependency vulnerability scanning, allowing PRs and pushes with committed secrets or vulnerable dependencies to merge without being blocked.

The `operon-guard` skill's preflight trust verification is self-defeating because its underlying `load_agent_from_path()` function fully executes untrusted agent code via `exec_module()` and class instantiation before any trust score is computed, meaning the security boundary it advertises is bypassed at the very step meant to enforce it.

Changing `reject` to `resolve` for failed exec outcomes breaks the `try/catch` error handling in callers like `bash-command.ts`, causing genuinely failed commands (e.g., timeouts or exit 127) to be incorrectly reported to the user as successes.

Removing the `OPENAI_DEFAULT_EMBEDDING_MODEL` import from `embeddings-openai.ts` leaves the constant unresolved, breaking all OpenAI-backed memory embedding functionality at runtime when the module is evaluated.

The `replaceSensitiveValuesInRaw` function fails to redact secrets in raw JSON snapshots when sensitive values contain special characters (e.g., newlines, backslashes) because it searches for literal characters against JSON-escaped text, causing confirmed credential leaks through the `raw` field.

When a best-effort delivery abort occurs inside `deliverOutboundPayloads()`, the error is swallowed and returns `result: undefined`, causing the `isAborted()` guard to be bypassed and `runCronIsolatedAgentTurn()` to incorrectly report `status: "ok"`, which suppresses retries and can mark timed-out one-shot jobs as successfully completed.

The `createGatewaySubagentRuntime.abort` method dispatches an `agent.abort` event that has no registered handler in the gateway method table (the correct handler is `chat.abort`), causing all subagent abort calls to fail at dispatch time instead of canceling the intended runs.

The guard in `activatePluginRegistry` that prevents overwriting the active registry also incorrectly blocks legitimate runtime workspace switches, causing subagent runs to retain a stale registry and leading to missing channel tools and "Unknown channel" failures for plugin channels belonging to the new workspace.

Catching and silencing all `resolveHooksConfig()` exceptions sets `hooksConfig` to `null` instead of failing fast, causing the gateway to boot in a broken half-enabled state where hook-adjacent services like Gmail watchers start successfully but inbound hook deliveries are silently dropped because no hook handler is mounted.

The inline query handler skips DM policy enforcement because `isSenderAllowed` treats an empty allowlist as permitted, allowing any Telegram user to trigger the LLM path even when `dmPolicy` is set to `"pairing"` or `"disabled"`.

When deriving the REST base URL, using only `parsed.hostname` drops any non-default port from `apiUrl`, causing REST calls to target the wrong origin and potentially leaking bearer tokens to an unintended service.

When `providerId` is `undefined`, provider-scoped `ownerAllowFrom` entries (e.g., `discord:owner-123`) are incorrectly added to the owner allowlist by stripping the prefix, allowing unintended owner privilege escalation in contexts where the provider cannot be resolved.

Newly forwarded fields (`clientTools`, `disableTools`, `streamParams`) in the agent RPC payload will cause runtime validation failures because the backend `AgentParamsSchema` uses `additionalProperties: false` and doesn't recognize them, silently breaking all structured subagent runs.

In `gateway-lock.ts`, prematurely returning `"dead"` when the lock owner's port is free — without verifying the PID belongs to a gateway process — allows a second process to steal the lock from a still-starting gateway, which then crashes with EADDRINUSE and deletes the stolen lock file, enabling a third gateway to start and resulting in two concurrent gateway processes running simultaneously.

In the smart-capture correction path, the old memory is deleted before the replacement is successfully persisted, meaning a failure in any subsequent async step (e.g., LLM call or DB write) will silently erase both the old and new facts with no way to recover them.

Deriving the ZK handoff capsule key from the same gateway RPC token used by all authenticated clients allows any client to forge valid capability proofs for arbitrary agent identities, completely breaking the handoff trust boundary.

The per-message retry fallback silently swallows send exceptions and only logs a debug message, allowing `flushPendingMessages` to resolve successfully with an empty result even when all delivery attempts fail, meaning `onError` is never triggered and the turn is falsely reported as settled.

The refactor removed the `listBundledWebSearchPluginIds` export from `bundled-web-search.ts`, but `src/secrets/runtime-web-tools.ts` still imports it, causing ESM binding failures and TypeScript errors that break web-tools runtime diagnostics.

DM pairing store approvals (`storeAllowFrom`) are incorrectly merged into the general `effectiveAllowFrom` list used to authorize room control commands, allowing users approved only for DM access to bypass room command authorization and execute control commands in any Matrix room.

A new no-config fallback in `resolveCommandAuthorizedFromAuthorizers` unconditionally returns `true` when no authorizers are configured, inadvertently allowing control commands from non-allowlisted DM senders by bypassing the existing DM access group gating contract.

Swallowing provider initialization failures silently creates a manager with a null provider, causing `sync()` to mark data as indexed without actually writing anything, leaving the FTS table empty and returning no results for keyword searches on fresh setups.

Hook-triggered model overrides (via `hooks.gmail.model` and `hooks.mappings[].model`) bypass private mode validation, allowing remote models like `openai/*` to be invoked even when the main agent config is enforced as local-only.

The new call to `isWorkspaceOnboardingCompleted` in `loadWorkspaceBootstrapFiles` references an undeclared and unimported symbol, causing a `ReferenceError` that will crash all agent bootstrap flows at runtime.

The `isSafePath()` function only performs a lexical path check and does not account for symlinks, allowing symbolic links inside the served directory to point to files outside the static root, which can then be read and served via `createReadStream()`.

The `handle_webhook` function is incomplete — its struct literal, match branch, and function body are never closed — preventing the Rust addon from compiling and blocking any build that cannot rely on a prebuilt binary.

Even though `skipAuthProfileClear` prevents clearing `authProfileOverride` inside `createModelSelectionState`, `runPreparedReply()` still calls `resolveSessionAuthProfileOverride` which deletes any saved auth profile whose provider differs from the current turn, meaning a single image sent through an `imageModel` on a different provider will silently wipe the user's explicitly selected auth profile and cause subsequent text turns to fall back to the default auth-profile order.

Enabling `shouldSyncHistoryMessage` causes Baileys to replay recent messages on reconnect, and because the only deduplication guard is an in-memory cache that is lost on restart or crash, messages processed just before a restart can be replayed through the full pipeline, triggering duplicate replies, hooks, or automations.

The `doctor --fix` cleanup incorrectly deletes active session write locks based solely on a 30-minute staleness threshold, even though locks can be legitimately held for up to ~24.8 days, because cross-process cleanup has no visibility into the in-process `HELD_LOCKS` registry, risking concurrent write corruption to session files.

The new logic incorrectly downgrades terminal failures to success when any payload precedes the last error, causing cron jobs with failed final actions to suppress retries and alerts while recording misleading success status.

The new `sessions.search` gateway handler passes hardcoded `requesterSessionKey: undefined` and `sandboxed: false` to `searchSessionsViaFts`, bypassing visibility checks and potentially exposing transcript snippets from sessions that should be hidden in sandboxed or subagent contexts.

The new fallback that returns `true` when no authorizers are configured is too broad — it unintentionally grants command access in DM and group channels where empty allowlists were meant to deny all commands, because those paths produce `{ configured: false }` authorizer entries that now incorrectly satisfy the allow condition.

The redirect mapping between the new run ID and original run ID is registered too late—only after all retry attempts fail—so if the redispatched child run completes quickly, its lifecycle completion event arrives before the mapping exists, gets dropped, and leaves the parent run permanently stuck in limbo.

The `scan()` function silently sends 47 adversarial payloads through the real agent function during safety checks, meaning agents with side effects like messaging, state writes, or paid API calls can cause unintended spam or quota consumption when the scan is run.

Removing the reverse-lookup branch causes already-resolved API key values (e.g., the plaintext value of `OPENAI_API_KEY`) to be written directly into `models.json` instead of being normalized back to their environment variable references, reintroducing the plaintext secret persistence bug fixed in #38757.

The startup migration deletes per-agent `sessions.json` files that post-migration discovery logic still depends on, causing migrated session stores to become invisible to combined-store callers immediately after the first migrating boot.

A debug `console.log` statement was left in `enqueueSystemEvent()`, which is called on all inbound traffic, causing user message content and session metadata to be unconditionally leaked to stdout in production, bypassing the repo's logger controls.

When writing per-channel OAG config overrides, the code incorrectly reads the baseline value from the global resolver instead of the existing channel-scoped config, causing per-channel budgets and retry limits to be overwritten with wrong values and rollbacks to restore incorrect previous state.
The password reuse check only validates against password history but not the current password hash, allowing users to "change" their password to the same value and bypass rotation requirements.

Using the stale `initialAuthProfileOverrideSource` value (captured before potential `clearSessionAuthProfileOverride` calls) to set `preserveUserPin` can incorrectly re-apply a `"user"` source when persisting `finalAuthProfileId`, effectively recreating a cleared user pin and causing subsequent turns to skip auto-rotation.

When `READ_CONTACTS` permission is absent and a `contactName` is provided alongside a formatting-only `phoneNumber` (e.g., `"()"`), the new fallback branch accepts the number, which normalizes to an empty string and causes the SMS query to match every thread, leaking messages from all conversations.

The approval gate for Discord-originated turns can be bypassed because `inputProvenance.sourceChannel` is sourced from untrusted caller-supplied request JSON rather than verified session or connection state, allowing any client to spoof a Discord origin and reach the restricted cross-channel approval path.

`hasExecApprovalClients()` incorrectly reads `connect.clientId` and passes the wrong object shape to `isWebchatClient()` instead of using `connect.client.id` and `connect.client.mode`, causing approval clients to never be recognized and approvals to incorrectly expire as `no-approval-route` rather than waiting for user action.

The new `static-files` request handler runs before authentication is enforced and never calls `authorizeHttpGatewayConnect`, allowing unauthenticated requests to read files from the configured static files root when gateway auth is enabled.

The newly added `BROWSER`, `SDKMAN_DIR`, and `NVM_DIR` environment variable restrictions are never enforced for gateway/node host executions because the runtime path only calls `isDangerousHostEnvVarName()` and never checks `isDangerousHostEnvOverrideVarName()`, rendering the security hardening ineffective for those execution paths.

When `reasoningLevel === "on"`, incomplete turns that produce only an `isReasoning` payload are not caught by the `payloads.length === 0` guard, so no `meta.error` is set, the fallback logic is never triggered, and reasoning-enabled sessions silently return no reply to the user.

Emitting a "usage" lifecycle event after the "end" event causes `server-chat` to recreate an `agentRunSeq` map entry that is never cleaned up, since only "end" and "error" events trigger cleanup, leading to a permanent memory leak per successful run on long-lived gateways.

The `mergeConfig` function now returns the raw `chunking.overlap` value instead of the previously computed clamped value, meaning configs where `overlap >= tokens` will no longer be normalized to `tokens - 1`, causing `chunkMarkdown()` to produce ever-growing chunks that duplicate text and can exceed embedding size limits.

The Matrix transport layer strips Authorization headers on cross-origin redirects but fails to also strip request bodies on 307/308 redirects, allowing sensitive POST/PUT payloads (messages, passwords, cryptographic keys) to be forwarded to attacker-controlled origins.

Restricted plugins can bypass the `allowPromptInjection=false` guard and modify the messages sent to the model by mutating `event.messages` in place, because the registry strips returned fields but does not deep-clone the event object passed to `before_llm_call` handlers.

The AES-GCM nonce tracker purges entries after 60 seconds, allowing previously used nonces to be reaccepted and potentially reused with the same key, which is a catastrophic cryptographic failure that completely breaks confidentiality and integrity guarantees.

In `AccessWitnessRecorder.generateProof()`, the agent is given the `gatewayKey` to sign `gatewayMac`, which collapses the trust boundary between prover and verifier and allows any compromised agent to forge gateway-signed proofs that `verifyAccessProof()` will accept as legitimate.

The `agentAbortControllers` field is never passed to `startGatewayMaintenanceTimers` at the call site, causing the new loop to iterate over `undefined` and throw a fatal error that can crash the gateway server on the first cleanup tick.

After this patch, `resolveTaskScriptPath()` resolves under `$OPENCLAW_HOME/.openclaw` instead of the OS home, causing `readScheduledTaskCommand()` to fail to locate pre-existing Windows Scheduled Task scripts and leaving the real gateway running while `stop`/`restart` falsely report success.

Hard-coding SMS and call log feature flags in `defaultConfig` eliminates the Play-flavor permission stripping mechanism, meaning the Google Play Store build artifact will now include restricted permissions (`SEND_SMS`, `READ_SMS`, `READ_CALL_LOG`) that violate store safety requirements.

Switching from a single global sequence counter to per-client sequence counters causes the counter to reset to 1 on reconnect while clients retain their previous `lastSeq`, silently suppressing gap detection and allowing critical event-driven state (e.g., pending executions, pairing approvals) to be lost after a transient disconnect.

The LSP message parser incorrectly uses `Content-Length` (a byte count) as a JavaScript string slice index, causing JSON parse failures and permanent buffer misalignment whenever server responses contain non-ASCII characters.

The unconditional regex stripping of collect-mode queue markers in `stripStructuralPrefixes()` allows a user to trigger live commands like `/stop` or `/new` simply by pasting a collect-mode prompt into chat, because the function is also used for inbound command detection without verifying the input actually originated from a trusted collect wrapper.

The `authenticateGovdossApiKey` function grants full operator-level access to any caller who provides a non-empty API key string without validating it against a store of legitimate keys, allowing an attacker to forge arbitrary credentials and gain unauthorized access.

The new NO_REPLY short-circuit added before delivery path selection causes `dispatchCronDelivery` to exit early without awaiting active descendants or applying fallback summaries, meaning runs where the parent is silent but subagents produce non-silent output will never deliver the descendants' results.

Unconditionally overwriting `context.agentAbortControllers[runId]` with a new `AbortController` when a run ID is reused causes the original in-flight run to lose its abort reference, making it impossible to cancel or expire via `agent.abort` or timeout logic.

Widening `allowedSensitiveKeys` to include all skill env keys causes a silent credential leak where a second skill's subprocesses incorrectly inherit the first skill's sensitive values (e.g., `GITHUB_TOKEN`) due to the first-wins behavior in `acquireActiveSkillEnvKey()` when two enabled skills share the same sensitive key name.

The new `$value` download branch calls `res.arrayBuffer()` and buffers the entire response in memory before checking `maxBytes`, bypassing the streaming size-limit guards in `fetchRemoteMedia` and risking memory spikes or crashes on oversized hosted content.

The `register()` function resolves the native addon path relative to `src/index.ts`, pointing to a non-existent `extensions/rust-plugin/src/native/index.cjs` file, which causes a runtime throw before registration completes and makes all Rust tools and routes unavailable.

Setting `ssrfPolicy: { dangerouslyAllowPrivateNetwork: false }` on the sandbox browser config blocks loopback (`127.0.0.1`) CDP connections, causing `isChromeReachable()` and `getChromeWebSocketUrl()` to fail before the bridge can connect to Chrome and breaking all sandbox browser sessions.

Group JIDs in WhatsApp outbound target resolution are unconditionally authorized without checking the configured `groupPolicy`, `groupAllowFrom`, or `allowFrom` allowlist, allowing an agent to send reactions or messages to any WhatsApp group and bypassing the intended security policy entirely.

The new `BindingToolsSchema` allows `tools.allow`/`tools.deny` to be declared on route bindings in the Zod schema, but the policy is never actually enforced because `ResolvedAgentRoute` doesn't propagate it and the tool-filtering pipeline ignores it, creating a silent security bypass where per-route tool restrictions appear valid but have no effect.

If an exception is thrown during shutdown in `drainPendingDecryptions()` or `threadBindingManager.stop()`, `releaseSharedClientInstance()` is never called, leaving the shared client state marked as started despite the underlying Matrix client being stopped, which can cause a long-lived gateway process to reuse a stale, non-syncing client on the next monitor start for the same account.

A raw API key from `skills.entries["nano-banana-pro"].apiKey` is being interpolated directly into LLM prompt text during inline image research, exposing the credential to the model, its output, and any downstream logs or history instead of being passed through secure secret/environment plumbing.

Adding the overly broad `trafficmanager.net` suffix to the bearer-token auth allowlist allows any attacker-controlled `*.trafficmanager.net` subdomain to exfiltrate the bot's Authorization token by triggering a 401/403 challenge on an allowlisted request.

The workflow triggers a paid Claude API job with write permissions for any user who mentions `@claude` in an issue or comment, allowing untrusted actors on public repositories to consume API budget and cause unintended write-side effects without any collaborator or maintainer authorization check.

The FormData multipart upload path in the OpenAI video generation provider uses an unguarded `fetchWithTimeout` call instead of the SSRF-protected transport, allowing redirect-based SSRF attacks to private IPs when `baseUrl` points to attacker-controlled infrastructure.

Navigation metadata (page title and URL) returned directly as trusted tool output allows an attacker-controlled page to inject prompt-manipulation text that steers the model simply by being navigated to, before any content sanitization occurs.

The hardened template's protected file write guard relies on "out-of-band confirmation" that the model cannot cryptographically verify, meaning an attacker can trivially bypass it by claiming confirmation already occurred via injected prompt or tool output.

`drainPendingDecryptions` silently exits after a fixed number of retry attempts even if encrypted events remain unresolved, causing the caller to incorrectly persist a clean shutdown and permanently drop events that were still awaiting decryption keys.

Reading the raw request body before installing the size and timeout guard allows unauthenticated clients to send oversized or slow-drip payloads that are fully buffered without limits, regressing the previous bounded request handling in HTTP mode.

When saving the slash command cache, the entire on-disk cache is overwritten with only the successfully registered teams, causing any team that failed registration due to a transient error to lose its previously cached tokens and rendering its existing slash command callbacks unable to authenticate after a restart.

The import of `clearSharedPluginRuntimeOptions` from a non-existent `shared-runtime-options.js` module will cause an ESM module-resolution error at load time, breaking gateway startup before any server code can execute.

Home-relative paths (e.g. `~/...`) in `privacy.rules` are not expanded before being passed to `path.resolve()`, causing them to resolve incorrectly, `readFileSync()` to fail, and custom privacy rules to be silently dropped in favor of built-in defaults — meaning user-defined secrets go unredacted.

The private-mode validator fails to check `channels.modelByChannel` overrides, allowing remote provider references (e.g., `openai/...`) to bypass local-only enforcement and leak conversation data to disallowed external providers for messages on matched channels.

When `agent.wait` retries are exhausted after a redispatch, the cleanup callback reschedules `resumeSubagentRun` without preserving `newRunId`, allowing a still-running child agent to be incorrectly classified as `resumable-fresh` and re-dispatched with a new idempotency key, causing duplicate agent executions and potentially duplicate tool side effects or user-visible replies.

The new `zaiProvider` object is never registered in `buildMediaUnderstandingRegistry`, making it unreachable at runtime and causing audio transcription calls to fail with a "Media provider not available: zai" error.

A production import in the Telegram extension reaches outside its package boundary via a relative path (`../../../src/...`) instead of using the approved `openclaw/plugin-sdk/*` surface, which violates import boundary rules and will cause the ingress chain to silently fail in standalone plugin installs where that path doesn't exist.

The OAuth token refresh no longer holds the file-level lock that serializes auth-store mutations, so a concurrent profile deletion or re-authentication can be silently overwritten when the in-flight `performOAuthRefresh()` later persists its stale credentials via `writeCredentialToAgentStore()`.

Replacing the `app-defaults.ts` import with `debug-connection.ts` removes `DEFAULT_CRON_FORM` and `DEFAULT_LOG_LEVEL_FILTERS`, which are still referenced later in the file, leaving unresolved identifiers that can prevent the Control UI from booting or cause a runtime crash.

When restarting, followup queue draining is gated on `flushAllInboundDebouncers()` returning a non-zero count, meaning sessions with pending followups enqueued via `enqueueFollowupRun()` but no active debouncers will skip `waitForFollowupQueueDrain()`, causing those queued messages to be silently discarded on reinitialization.

Treating all dotless hostnames as unconditionally blocked causes single-label HTTP hosts (e.g., `http://synapse:8008`) to bypass IP-resolution and private-network validation, allowing them to be accepted without `allowPrivateNetwork` even when they resolve to public addresses in environments with DNS search domains or split-horizon DNS.

Interaction-triggered navigations (clicks, form submissions, evaluate) in `navigation-guard.ts` fail to install Playwright route interception handlers, allowing attacker-controlled pages to bypass strict SSRF protections by triggering requests to internal services through redirect chains before any validation occurs.

After a successful redispatch, the code stores `redirectedToRunId` but fails to complete the original run or clear its resume lock, leaving the parent entry permanently in-flight and causing downstream logic to incorrectly count it as an unfinished pending descendant.
The new import block in `channel.ts` reintroduces symbols already imported elsewhere in the file, causing `TS2300 duplicate identifier` errors that prevent the Slack channel module from building.

In `updateManagedSkills`, skill files are written to disk before `enforceManagedScanPolicy` is called, so a "blocked" scan result fails to prevent unsafe code from being installed since the files already exist on the filesystem.

The cleanup logic only removes order entries present in `toRemove` (built from existing `store.profiles` keys), so stale IDs already missing from `store.profiles` are never pruned from `store.order`, allowing `resolveAuthProfileOrder` to treat a dangling-ID-only order as authoritative and return no usable profiles even when valid ones exist.

Discord accounts with an `agentId` but no token are incorrectly included in synthesized route bindings, causing the agent to auto-select them for outbound message delivery and ultimately throwing a runtime error when the missing token is required to create the Discord client.

The gateway guard generates an `approvalId` and continuation for approval-required requests but never persists them to `govdossApprovalStore` or the paused executor registry, making every generated approval permanently unresolvable by the `/approve`, `/reject`, and `/resume` endpoints.

The ACP command handler uses a `sendNodeEvent` helper that silently swallows transport errors, meaning failed event deliveries are treated as successes, which can leave runs permanently stuck in recovery with inconsistent replay and cancel semantics.

The Discord provider module imports `forgetDiscordManagedBotIdentity` and `rememberDiscordManagedBotIdentity` from `../accounts.js`, but neither symbol is exported from that file, causing ESM import resolution to fail at startup and preventing the Discord monitor from running.

Calling `loadAuthProfileStore()` to inspect a secondary agent's credentials unexpectedly triggers `syncExternalCliCredentials()`, which can silently copy unrelated third-party OAuth secrets into the target agent's store before the user is prompted to reuse any credentials.

In the shared-workspace path, skill filters from multiple agents are merged together, allowing an agent to inherit and expose skills that were only allowlisted by a co-tenant agent, potentially surfacing invalid or unauthorized command entries for each individual agent.

The `safeFetch` function is vulnerable to DNS rebinding SSRF attacks because it validates a URL's resolved IP in a separate DNS lookup from the actual `fetch()` call, allowing an attacker-controlled domain with a short TTL to pass the blocklist check but resolve to a private/internal IP during the real request.

The 10-minute inline query cleanup deletes the map entry but never resolves or rejects `inlineMessageIdPromise`, leaving the `Promise.all` chain permanently pending and leaking captured state for every timed-out query, causing unbounded memory growth under normal inline typing usage.

The normal-closure pairing fallback guard only checks `device-auth.json` and fails to exclude shared-auth sessions (e.g., `--token`/`--password`), allowing a paired operator with limited scopes like `operator.pairing` to approve a pending request requiring elevated scopes such as `operator.admin` via the local `approveDevicePairing()` path, which skips the scope enforcement applied by the gateway RPC path.

After a successful redispatch, the code stores `redirectedToRunId` but never completes the original run or registers a waiter for the new run ID, causing the parent run to remain permanently in-flight and blocking descendant-settlement and announce cleanup.

Trigger session settings (`contextMode`, `modelOverride`, `maxTokens`, `thinking`) are saved directly on `NodeData` by the editor but read from a nonexistent nested `trigger.data.sessionConfig`, causing user-configured model and context options to be silently dropped and replaced with defaults at execution time.

When a stop-then-start sequence occurs during an in-flight startup, the new `startChannel()` call silently waits for the aborted startup promise and exits without ever re-invoking `startAccount()`, causing the channel to remain permanently stopped.

A missing `command` argument in `ExecApprovalsGatewayPrompter.fallbackDecision` causes the new exact-match args comparison to always fail, silently denying previously allowlisted commands in headless or inactive sessions where prompts cannot be shown.

A 100ms memoization cache on session entry lookups causes `isFollowupRunSuperseded()` and `shouldSuppressOutboundDelivery` to read stale session data, allowing a superseded agent run to bypass suppression checks and leak a partial or final reply after the session has already been replaced.

Removing `matrixSetupWizard` and `matrixSetupAdapter` from the plugin-sdk matrix module breaks existing public API contracts, causing import failures for any plugins or tooling compiled against the current published entrypoints.

Channel post command authorization ignores per-channel `groupPolicy` overrides, so channels explicitly configured with `groupPolicy: disabled` or `allowlist` can still execute native/plugin commands because the auth check only verifies the presence of a `groupConfig` entry rather than evaluating the channel's actual policy.

The variable `vagrant_status` is never initialized on the first-create path, causing the script to immediately crash with an "unbound variable" error under `set -u` right after a successful initial provision when `[[ -n "$vagrant_status" ]]` is evaluated.

The new `talk.ptt.*` commands invoked by `handlePTTCommand` are never added to the gateway's allowlist, causing all `/ptt` requests to be rejected with "command not allowlisted" on a default configuration, making the feature completely unusable without manual operator intervention.

The unconditional `CommandLane.Cron` queue check causes `wakeMode: "now"` manual cron runs to immediately return `"cron-in-progress"` and get permanently skipped, because the busy-wait retry loop in `timer.ts` only retries on `"requests-in-flight"`, not `"cron-in-progress"`.

By aggregating routes from a global registry pool instead of scoping to the current `params.registry`, the gateway HTTP handler inadvertently exposes plugin routes from other agents' workspaces, allowing foreign or stale webhook endpoints to leak across workspace boundaries.

The dynamic import for the native addon uses a relative path (`./native/index.cjs`) that resolves incorrectly from `src/index.ts`, pointing to a non-existent file instead of the actual loader at `extensions/rust-plugin/native/index.cjs`, causing the plugin to throw an error and fail to initialize before any tools are registered.

The `resolveCronCallerOptions` function relies on a `callerSessionKey` from request params, but the cron schema's `additionalProperties: false` blocks that field from ever being passed, causing `callerOwnsJob` to fall back to an allow-all mode and leaving cross-session cron job access fully unguarded despite the intended isolation fix.

The workflow serializer saves Supabase node payloads under UI-only keys (`row`, `updates`, `function`, `paramsData`, `supabaseInstance`) that don't match the keys the executor expects (`data`, `functionName`, `args`, `instance`), causing INSERT/UPDATE/RPC nodes to fail at runtime with missing payloads and always fall back to the default Supabase instance.

The configured `embedding.dimensions` value is used to size the LanceDB schema but is never forwarded to the embeddings API call, causing a vector shape mismatch at runtime when a non-default dimension is set.

The `strictInlineEval` security gate can be bypassed when versioned interpreter binaries (e.g., `python3.11`, `node20`) or symlink-resolved paths are used, because `findInterpreterSpec` relies on exact name matching against a static list that omits versioned variants, allowing persistent "allow-always" entries to be created contrary to the feature's documented guarantee.

When `spawnMode === "session"`, registering the initial streamed ACP task as a subagent run causes the session status to be permanently pinned to that first ended run, making the sessions API and UI incorrectly report `done` while subsequent follow-up messages in the same ACP thread are still actively running.

Registering every Feishu account's bot name under the shared `"feishu"` key causes each new account to overwrite the previous one, so in multi-account deployments all agent hooks will resolve to the last-registered bot name instead of the correct per-account identity.

Applying the session-lane timeout to `runTurn()` in `withSessionActor` incorrectly overrides the existing turn-timeout policy, causing any ACP turn exceeding ~10 minutes to fail with `ACP_TURN_FAILED` instead of respecting the intended 48-hour (or unlimited) turn timeout.

The automatic rollback logic only triggers when the config file is marked invalid at snapshot time, but it fails to handle a later startup failure caused by unresolved required secrets in `prepareGatewayStartupConfig()`, leaving the process to exit without attempting a rollback to a previously working config.

The `resolveCronCallerOptions` function always returns `callerSessionKey: undefined`, causing the new ownership guard to treat all non-admin requests as if the caller is anonymous and bypass filtering, so non-admin clients can still retrieve all job names via `cron.runs` with `scope: "all"` — leaving the intended job-name information leak fix ineffective.

Four compatibility-related exports (`buildPluginCompatibilityNotices`, `buildPluginCompatibilityWarnings`, `formatPluginCompatibilityNotice`, and `summarizePluginCompatibility`) were removed from `status.ts` but are still imported by CLI command surfaces, causing ESM module load failures that prevent those commands from starting.

When a streaming preview POST or PATCH request times out via `Promise.race`, the underlying HTTP request is never cancelled, allowing it to complete late and either create an orphaned draft post or silently overwrite the already-finalized message due to stale `streamMessageId` state.

Sourcing the `.env` file containing a user-supplied `VM_PASSWORD` in `cmd_status` allows arbitrary shell code execution if the password contains command substitution syntax like `$(...)` or backticks, and should be parsed safely instead of evaluated.

The Feishu plugin removed handler implementations for several advertised actions (pin, unpin, list-pins, member-info, channel-info, channel-list, and reaction actions), causing users who invoke those actions to always hit runtime failures despite the plugin still advertising them as available.

Replacing the hardcoded `OPENCLAW_PLUGIN_API_VERSION` of `"1.2.0"` with the package version (e.g., `2026.3.23`) breaks semver compatibility checks, causing all existing ClawHub plugins advertising a `^1.2.0` API range to be incorrectly rejected as incompatible until their metadata is migrated.

The `modelFallbacks` logic only triggers when model resolution fails, not when the provider endpoint is actually unreachable at runtime, so compaction failures due to downed servers (the primary use case) will never attempt any configured fallback models.

When the pending-inbound drain queue reaches its 200-entry cap, dropped messages are only logged at verbose level and Telegram still receives a success response, causing silent message loss that operators cannot detect under normal logging configurations.

Storing user-specific configuration files inside the skill directory causes them to be bundled into the distributable `.skill` package, potentially leaking a maintainer's local settings or credentials to downstream users.

Doctor's `scanExecSafeBinCoverage` uses a fixtures-only profile merge that excludes built-in `SAFE_BIN_PROFILES`, causing `doctor --fix` to falsely flag built-in-covered binaries as unprotected and scaffold empty `{}` profiles that silently override and strip their built-in security constraints.

When a queued delivery has no explicit account ID, the recovery logic falls back to the hardcoded key "default" instead of resolving the channel's actual default account ID, causing those entries to always appear disconnected and never be retried.

The audit check incorrectly suppresses a wildcard authorization finding when a top-level `groupAllowFrom` restriction exists, producing a false negative because the Telegram runtime replaces (not intersects) the top-level allowlist with per-group overrides, leaving groups with `allowFrom: ["*"]` effectively unrestricted.

The `<tool_call>` tag recovery fallback indiscriminately parses any tag-shaped snippet in plain-text responses, meaning example or explanatory tool-call markup in the model's reply will be silently executed as real tool invocations and stripped from the visible output.

When `includeMms=true`, the new by-phone MMS search path buffers up to `offset + limit` messages in memory with no upper bound on offset, allowing a caller to trigger an OOM or device hang by requesting a large page offset on a long message thread.

The `dangerous-exec-python` regex only matches `os.system`/`os.popen`/`os.exec*` calls with literal string arguments, allowing malicious code to bypass this critical security check by passing the payload through a variable instead.

When `state_update_systemd()` increments the health generation mid-flight, a completing `gateway probe` exits early without calling `state_set_probe_in_flight(FALSE)`, permanently locking the deep-probe lane and freezing all subsequent diagnostics until the app is restarted.

Removing `resolvePinnedHostnameWithPolicy` from the non-pinned DNS code path inadvertently bypasses all SSRF hostname and IP validation, allowing callers who set `pinDns: false` to reach blocked or private targets without triggering a `SsrFBlockedError`.

The new `input.signal?.aborted` branch in `runTurn()` incorrectly calls `killProcessTree(child)` on turn cancellation, SIGTERMing the persistent session's owner process that should survive for reuse, causing warm-session loss and potential dead-session recovery on the next turn.

The metadata-based version fallback triggers on any spawn error (e.g., EACCES, bad shebang, missing Node interpreter), causing `checkAcpxVersion()` to incorrectly return `ok: true` and skip the repair install, leaving the ACP backend permanently broken instead of self-healing.

Expanding `isToolCallBlock()` to recognize `toolCall`/`functionCall` types causes `validateAnthropicTurns()` to strip OpenAI-style tool call blocks before `sanitizeToolUseResultPairing()` can repair them, orphaning the corresponding `toolResult` messages and causing providers like Moonshot to silently lose valid tool context.

The bare-JSON and `<tool_call>` XML-wrapper regexes in `extractMarkdownToolCalls` both match the same response pattern, causing duplicate tool call entries that result in the tool executing twice downstream in `buildAssistantMessage()`.

Adding `"tavily"` as a valid literal in the Zod schema without wiring it into `resolveSearchProvider` causes any Tavily configuration to be silently ignored and fall back to Brave, resulting in unexpected authentication failures for users who configure Tavily as their web search provider.

The `resolveCronCallerOptions` function fails to populate `callerAgentId` in the gateway caller options, causing agent-owned cron jobs (those with `agentId` set but no `sessionKey`) to be incorrectly rejected as unauthorized during scoped `cron.list`, `cron.update`, `cron.remove`, and `cron.run` requests, even for the rightful owner.

The new reply hint instructs agents to use `sessions_send` for private replies but omits the required `deliveryMode: "private"` or `announce: false` flags, so agents following the hint will default to public announcement mode and inadvertently leak private replies into public channels or trigger nested announce loops.

The credential refresh logic only persists updated OAuth tokens when the expiry timestamp increases, but since providers can rotate access and refresh tokens without changing the expiry, the old (potentially invalidated) tokens remain in the store, causing repeated failed refresh attempts with a stale refresh token on subsequent requests.

Removing `!hasRequestScopeClient` from the condition allows the fallback model-override authorization path to execute even when a real gateway client is present, enabling non-admin, request-scoped callers to bypass normal override restrictions via plugin trust settings.

Making the Fly entrypoint script executable and implicitly the default for all container launches breaks rootless Podman deployments, because the entrypoint attempts to write to paths like `/data`, `/root`, and `/usr/local/bin` that a non-root user lacks permission to modify, causing the container to exit before the application ever starts.

The durable state skip list is built from `.env` key names alone without checking for empty or placeholder values, so variables like `OPENAI_API_KEY=` incorrectly block valid shell values from being copied into the service environment, causing provider authentication failures at runtime.

The `hasExplicitNonStringGatewayCredentialForMode` function fails to recognize env-template SecretRefs (e.g., `"${TOKEN}"`) as explicit credentials because it only checks for non-string (object-form) SecretRefs, causing auto-generation to silently overwrite user-configured string-typed SecretRef tokens with plaintext generated values when the referenced environment variable is absent at startup.

When all queued items have already been sent, the early-exit `break` skips the dropped-summary cleanup, leaving `droppedCount` non-zero and causing the `finally` block to reschedule the drain indefinitely without ever delivering the overflow summary.

The desktop splash screen hardcodes the gateway probe URL without accounting for the configured `basePath`, causing the health check to always fail and leaving the app stuck on the loading screen when `gateway.controlUi.basePath` is set.

On the non-snapshot path, `allowedSensitiveKeys` is built from all raw workspace skill entries rather than only the skills active for the current run, potentially whitelisting sensitive environment variables like `OPENAI_API_KEY` and `GITHUB_TOKEN` in the sandbox on behalf of disabled or ineligible skills.

The `isSameOAuthIdentity()` function fails to compare `accountId`, so two different OAuth accounts sharing the same `profileId` but no email can be incorrectly treated as identical, allowing one agent to overwrite another account's tokens and causing agents to authenticate as the wrong user.

Reading the request body before installing the body limit guard removes byte-limit and timeout protections, allowing oversized or slow-drip requests to exhaust memory and hold connections open on the Slack HTTP endpoint.

Passing `onlyPluginIds` to the second `loadGatewayPlugins()` call triggers a full global reload in `loadOpenClawPlugins()`, wiping out all non-deferred plugins' hooks, commands, and providers from the registry for the remainder of runtime.

The deduplication check drops redelivered messages that arrive while the first attempt is still in-flight, and since the error handler only clears the cache key for future deliveries without replaying the already-dropped retry, a message can be permanently lost if the first attempt fails before Discord sends another copy.

When a non-default agent resolves the special key `"current"`, the new `sessions.resolve` gateway call canonicalizes it against the default-store agent rather than the requester, causing session operations like `sessions_history` and `sessions_send` to silently target an unrelated session belonging to the default agent instead of the caller's own session.

The self-reset deadlock guard in `sessions-manage-tool.ts` can be bypassed because `resolvedKey` and `effectiveRequesterKey` are compared without normalization, allowing a canonical main session calling `sessions_manage` with the `"main"` alias to slip past the check and still trigger the dangerous `sessions.reset` path.

The updated masking logic for 5–6 character secrets now exposes up to 80% of the value (e.g., `abcde` → `abcd...`) instead of masking it more aggressively as before (e.g., `a...e`), creating a security regression wherever `maskApiKey()` is used to display secrets in chat or command output.

When a timed-out attempt has already committed external side effects (e.g., sending a message or adding a cron job), unconditionally retrying via `continue` can replay those side effects and cause duplicate actions such as resent messages or duplicate scheduled jobs.

When a contact name search yields no matching phone numbers, the code fails to short-circuit and instead queries all SMS messages without an address filter, leaking unrelated SMS history to the caller.

Skipping exec refs entirely before `collectDryRunResolvabilityErrors()` runs causes dry-run to falsely report success for broken configurations (e.g., missing provider aliases or source mismatches) that would fail at runtime, because static provider validation that occurs before any exec command is launched is never performed on the skipped refs.

Once a stream is registered, `playTts()` unconditionally skips the TwiML `<Say>` fallback for calls without telephony TTS configured, causing all audio responses to silently fail on deployments that rely on that fallback path.

The proxy-selection branch fails to honor the `dangerouslyAllowPrivateNetwork` policy flag, causing callers who rely on that alias to be incorrectly routed through `EnvHttpProxyAgent`, which can leak private/local network requests to the proxy or cause failures unless `NO_PROXY` is explicitly configured.

Inlining `runtimeContext` during overflow recovery drops critical fields (e.g., `currentChannelId`, `bashElevated`, `reasoningLevel`, `extraSystemPrompt`) that compaction relies on, causing delegated compaction to run under a different context than manual or after-turn compaction and potentially producing incorrect summaries or missing actions.

When a turn is aborted via `input.signal`, the new code incorrectly kills the entire `prompt` process tree, destroying the persistent ACPX session that `cancelSession()` expects to remain alive and reusable, causing subsequent turns to lose conversation state.

The OAuth token refresh logic was refactored to only handle hardcoded providers, removing the plugin hook that allowed extension providers with custom `refreshOAuth` implementations to renew their tokens, causing permanent refresh failures for those providers after expiry.

The new fallback logic for `targetStrictMode` incorrectly assigns `false` for direct Azure OpenAI (`*.openai.azure.com`) endpoints, causing `shouldStripOpenAIStrictFlag()` to strip the `strict` field from tool definitions before the Azure allowlist can run, which regresses structured tool calling for Azure users.

In `acp-reset-target.ts`, ACP bindings with a missing or empty `accountId` incorrectly match all accounts instead of only the default account, which can cause reset, steer, or cancel operations on non-default accounts (e.g., Slack, WhatsApp, IRC) to target the wrong agent session.

Because `currentConfig` is advanced even when plugin changes are skipped during hot reload, a subsequent unrelated hot-reload can silently apply stale plugin policy changes (such as model allowlists) without the required restart, bypassing the intended restart boundary.

With `set -u` enforcing strict unbound variable checks, internal variables like `DOCKER_SOCKET_PATH` are dereferenced before being assigned defaults, causing the script to immediately abort and making the entire Docker setup entrypoint unusable.

When no `ref` is provided, the genome reference resolution unconditionally falls back to a Voltaris V2 fixture directory that is not included in the published package, causing `openclaw genome` commands to fail in packaged environments.

The updated audit finding misleadingly suggests that mention gating reduces risk in open LINE rooms with elevated tools enabled, when in fact `resolveMentionGatingWithBypass()` only enforces the gate when `canDetectMention` is true and LINE's handler bypasses it for non-text messages and authorized control commands, leaving the prompt injection risk materially understated.

The tombstone guard only clears reused run keys on `lifecycle/start` or `seq === 1`, so if the gateway first sees a reused key at seq 2 or later (e.g., the start event was lost), all subsequent events for that run are silently dropped, causing the chat to never stream or finalize.

The new `closeAll()` method clears `INDEX_CACHE_PENDING` without awaiting in-flight manager creation promises, allowing those promises to later re-insert live managers with open FSWatcher handles into the already-cleared cache, defeating the existing race-safe teardown logic.

The `baseTokenFields.length > 0` guard incorrectly injects a `default` account even when the base config is only partially authenticated, causing `resolveDefaultAccountId()` to select an unconfigured account over valid named accounts for channels like Slack and Mattermost that require additional fields beyond a token to be runnable.

The "new installs only" guard is too weak because existing configs missing the `agents.defaults.memorySearch` key will also satisfy the preconditions after `applyAgentDefaults()` runs, causing current users to be unexpectedly opted into experimental session memory, MMR, and temporal decay features upon upgrading.

A process-global `lastCancelReason` variable is shared across concurrent compaction sessions, meaning under normal multi-session load one session can overwrite another's cancellation reason before it is read, causing `/compact` and overflow recovery to report the wrong root cause.

The new `hasSameHost()` fast-path in `resolveUniqueEndpointId` incorrectly collapses distinct Azure project endpoints sharing the same hostname into a single provider entry, causing the second project to overwrite the first's `baseUrl` and orphaning any models registered under the original project.

When the GLM-4.7 template model is absent from the registry, spreading `null` into the synthesized GLM-5 forward-compat model leaves `baseUrl` undefined, causing the OpenAI SDK to fall back to `https://api.openai.com/v1` and misroute ZAI credentials to OpenAI's servers.

The `subagent.abort` implementation dispatches an `agent.abort` gateway call that has no registered handler or advertised method, causing all plugin-driven abort attempts to silently fail with an "unknown method" error and leaving subagent runs uncancel-able.

When a dynamic model wins the preference check over an already-resolved entry, `applyConfiguredProviderOverrides` rebuilds it from the plugin template without copying the discovered `baseUrl`, silently reverting requests to the default endpoint and breaking deployments that route traffic through a custom proxy configured in `models.json`.

Changing `delivered` from `true` to `false` for silent reply tokens causes hook-dispatched agent runs to incorrectly post a fallback "NO_REPLY" summary to the main session instead of remaining silent, reintroducing a silent-token leak for shared delivery-contract callers.

Temporarily swapping `globalThis.fetch` around `executeRequest` is not concurrency-safe, as overlapping calls from multiple Discord accounts running in parallel via `Promise.all` can interleave the save/restore of `globalThis.fetch`, causing requests to be routed through the wrong proxy or leaving `globalThis.fetch` permanently overwritten with a proxy.
Returning `wakeOptions` without `sessionKey` for cron sessions removes agent scoping, causing heartbeat wakes to execute across all heartbeat-enabled agents instead of only the originating one.

The removal of the `CLLocationManager.locationServicesEnabled()` guard from `currentPermissions()` causes the gateway to incorrectly report location as available even when system-wide Location Services are disabled, leading to failed location requests at runtime.

The `url` field in the OpenBrain plugin config is not marked as sensitive, meaning API keys embedded in the URL via the documented `?key=` auth pattern will bypass config redaction and be exposed in snapshots and UI flows.

The restart deferral logic emits SIGUSR1 immediately without waiting for async pre-restart hooks to complete, causing race conditions where the restart sentinel may not be written before the process restarts, leading to lost restart notifications on startup.

Spreading `resolveAllProxyFallbackOptions()` after the existing proxy options inadvertently overwrites the `noProxy` loopback exemptions set by `applyGlobalProxyDispatcher()`, causing local model endpoint requests (e.g., Ollama on `127.0.0.1`) to be incorrectly routed through the proxy on every call to `ensureGlobalUndiciStreamTimeouts()` during embedded runs.

The private mode validator fails to check `tools.media` model configurations, allowing media files to be silently uploaded to remote providers like OpenAI or Google even when the deployment otherwise passes private mode validation.

The new `hookContext` hard-codes the transient `messageChannelId` for Discord DMs instead of the stable `user:${author.id}` key used by the inbound path, causing `message_sent` hooks to be attributed to a different conversation than `message_received` events and breaking per-conversation plugins on all Discord direct-message replies.

Changing the SMS permission check from `&&` to `||` causes the onboarding flow to incorrectly treat SMS as fully granted when only one of `SEND_SMS` or `READ_SMS` is granted, permanently blocking the user from being prompted for the missing permission.

The new route matcher can find routes from pooled registries, but the gateway auth precheck only consults the original registry, causing requests to pooled routes requiring gateway auth to be incorrectly rejected and fall through to 404 handling instead of being authenticated and routed to the plugin.

Removing `formatMentionForText` (and `buildMentionedMessage`) from `mention.ts` breaks the Feishu package entrypoint because `index.ts` still re-exports both symbols, causing a missing-export module linking error before any runtime code can execute.

In `releaseLock()`, the lock file is never closed before `fs.rm()` is called, causing Windows to reject the unlink with a silently swallowed `EPERM` error, which leaves stale lock files behind and forces subsequent lock acquisitions to wait for the full TTL or timeout even after the previous operation has completed.
In QMD deployments, `memory_get` incorrectly uses a status-only manager whose `readFile()` always throws, breaking all memory file retrieval for that backend.

The memory flush run partially replays stored sender/source metadata but still reads `messageProvider` and `agentAccountId` from the live session context, meaning if the session's channel or account changes between the original user turn and the deferred flush, hook policy and message-tool calls could target the wrong channel or account.

When all batched documents exceed the size limit, the caption text still incorrectly falls through to the agent queue despite no files being successfully processed, causing the agent to respond to a message without ever receiving its attachments.

When the selected unit changes generation while a health probe is in-flight, an early return fails to release the in-flight lock via `state_set_health_in_flight(FALSE)`, permanently blocking all future health probes for the session and causing the tray to silently display stale status instead of reporting degraded or config-warning states.

Wrapping `register(api)` inside an `if (shouldActivate)` block causes non-activating plugin loads to skip registration entirely, resulting in empty registries that break provider resolution, web-search discovery, and runtime hooks for callers like `resolvePluginProviders()` that rely on non-activating loads to populate snapshot registries.

Replacing the wildcard `export *` in `runtime-api.ts` with a single named type export drops critical runtime exports (e.g., `createActionCard`, `processLineMessage`, `CardAction`) that `extensions/line/api.ts` depends on, causing LINE plugin loading to fail.

The required `groups` argument has been removed from both `createWhatsAppPluginBase` call sites in the WhatsApp channel package, causing a missing required property type error that will prevent the package from compiling or loading.

Hardcoding `sessionTarget: "main"` in `runAgentAttempt` overrides legitimate non-main session keys (e.g., cron sessions), causing `handleAgentEnd` to misclassify cron-session failures as main-session failures and breaking the provenance tracking this change intended to introduce.

The `TASK_CONFIG_ROOT_ENV_KEYS` whitelist omits `HOME` and `USERPROFILE`, causing `resolveTaskConfigEnvironment()` to retain the shell's config root instead of the task's overridden home directory, which leads `resolveConfiguredGatewayPort()` to read the wrong config file and target the wrong gateway port during stop/restart operations.

In-flight OAuth refresh requests are deduplicated by `profileId` alone, so two concurrent callers with different OAuth identities that share the same `profileId` will incorrectly collapse onto one refresh promise, causing the second caller to receive the wrong account's API key.

Updating `lastSeen` before reply generation or message sending succeeds causes unread DMs to be silently dropped on transient failures, since the next poll incorrectly treats the conversation as already handled.

The new `sessionsList` options added to the `GatewayConfig` type are not reflected in the Zod runtime validation schema, which uses strict mode, causing config validation to fail and preventing startup when these documented keys are used.

When a force-cycle restart occurs, the `finally` block skips awaiting `runner.stop()`/`bot.stop()`, allowing a new bot instance to start before in-flight update handlers from the old cycle finish, which causes duplicate update processing and duplicate user-visible replies due to re-fetched updates from the last persisted offset.

The edit tool's description unconditionally advertises `apply_patch` as a fallback, but `apply_patch` is only added to the tool list under specific conditions (feature flag enabled and OpenAI provider/model), meaning non-OpenAI and default sessions will have the model instructed to use a tool that doesn't exist, likely causing infinite retry loops.

Deduplicating providers by `envVar` causes the `openai` provider to always be dropped in favor of `openai-codex` when both share `OPENAI_API_KEY`, so any explicitly configured `openai/*` model gets incorrectly overwritten with an `openai-codex` model at startup.

An unconditional `node.list` call added to the unfiltered `nodes list` command now requires `operator.read` scope in addition to `operator.pairing`, breaking tokens that were intentionally scoped to pairing-only access and causing unauthorized errors where previously they would succeed.

The newly added `fromCronLane: true` flag intended to prevent self-blocking in the cron lane is silently dropped by intermediate layers (`CronServiceDeps.runHeartbeatOnce` and the gateway adapter) before reaching `runHeartbeatOnce`, causing manual cron runs with `wakeMode: "now"` to still hit the `cron-in-progress` block, time out, and fall back to queued wake instead of firing an immediate heartbeat.
The monitor-bypass early return is evaluated before the `groupPolicy: "disabled"` check, allowing group messages to be silently processed even when an admin has explicitly disabled all group traffic.

Because `chat.send` now seeds `minEventTsByEffectiveRunKey` for every request but non-streaming/no-agent-run paths never call `clearEffectiveChatRunState`, each completed idempotency key leaves a permanent map entry, causing an unbounded memory leak on long-lived gateways.

The new socket-address fallback in `isLocalDirectRequest` incorrectly classifies Tailscale Serve proxied clients (with `remoteAddress=::1` but non-loopback `X-Forwarded-For`) as local-direct, which suppresses Tailscale header authentication in `authorizeGatewayConnect` and breaks ws-control-ui auth for Serve setups relying on Tailscale identity.

The mention-stripping regex uses `[:\s]*` as a separator, allowing zero-length matches that can incorrectly rewrite non-command messages like `@bot:example.org/new` into `/new`, potentially triggering unintended command execution.

The ClawAPI provider catalog imports internal helpers directly from `../../src/...` instead of the approved `openclaw/plugin-sdk/provider-models` boundary, causing a module-not-found error at runtime in packaged installs where the `src` tree is absent and preventing ClawAPI from initializing.

When updating an existing Azure custom model, spreading `nextModel` overwrites the entire `compat` object with only `{supportsStore: false}`, silently discarding any previously set compatibility overrides (e.g., `thinkingFormat`, tool quirks) and potentially causing protocol-level failures on subsequent onboarding runs.

The `cmdExeCommandLine` computation and its unsafe-argument validation are now applied unconditionally to all `runExec` calls instead of only within the `.cmd`/`.bat` wrapper branch, causing legitimate arguments like URL query strings or percent-encoded values to incorrectly throw `Unsafe Windows cmd.exe argument detected` and prevent process execution.

When the first message in an unknown group is a slash command, the `unknownGroupAction` logic is bypassed entirely because command middleware runs before and independently of the group-check handler, meaning bots configured to warn or leave unknown groups will instead silently remain in them.

The private mode policy check only validates the provider ID (e.g., "ollama") without inspecting the configured `baseUrl`, allowing prompts and embeddings to be silently routed to remote endpoints and bypassing the intent of private mode entirely.

The new branch incorrectly increments the shared-secret rate-limit bucket for device-token-only authentication flows where no shared secret was ever attempted, which can cause legitimate clients to be rate-limited on unrelated logins after enough reconnects.

When a file rename fails with ENOENT (indicating the entry was already acknowledged), the entry is skipped in the rename step but never removed from the `eligible` array, causing it to still be processed in the subsequent delivery loop and potentially re-sending already-delivered messages to users.

The fallback detection compares against a `provider`/`model` that may have already been overwritten to the fallback values on a prior retry, causing `isFromFallback` to incorrectly return `false` on subsequent turns and allowing the fallback model to be incorrectly persisted as the sticky runtime model.

When a `lane: "cron"` run is aborted or expires, cleanup incorrectly targets the raw `"cron"` lane instead of the resolved `"nested"` lane stored in `clearableLanes`, leaving stale active-task state that can permanently block that lane.

A hardcoded 5-second timeout deletes the over-limit message preview before the authoritative `deliverMattermostReplyPayload()` resend is confirmed successful, risking permanent message loss if the resend takes longer than 5 seconds or fails entirely.

Because `bestEffortDeliver` defaults to `true` for main-session agent runs, the new catch block silently suppresses `INVALID_REQUEST` errors for all routing misconfigurations—not just webchat exec-approval followups—causing broken channel setups to appear successful instead of returning errors to CLI or automation callers.

Passing `agentId` into `syncSkillsToWorkspace()` for a shared sandbox causes whichever agent initializes last to overwrite the shared `skills/` directory for all agents, meaning other agents can lose their allowed skills or inherit the wrong set.

The `shouldInjectBedrockBearerWrapper` function only checks `authOverride` but ignores profile-based auth resolution, causing `AWS_BEARER_TOKEN_BEDROCK` to always inject an `Authorization: Bearer` header that can override valid profile-resolved credentials whenever the env var is set, rendering explicit profile selection ineffective.

When an unlocker zone doesn't exist, the bootstrap path automatically creates it with `ub_premium: true`, which silently enables higher-cost billing for premium domains on first use without any user opt-in or warning.

The new `z.preprocess` silently converts large numeric Discord snowflakes to incorrect string values due to JavaScript's floating-point precision limits, persisting corrupted IDs instead of rejecting them as the previous validation did.

The new TTL sweep branch unconditionally calls `safeRemoveAttachmentsDir` on all expired runs, ignoring the `retainAttachmentsOnKeep` flag, causing attachments to be deleted even for `cleanup: "keep"` runs where they were explicitly meant to be preserved.

Replacing the `openclaw/plugin-sdk/*` imports with relative paths causes runtime module resolution failures because the referenced files don't exist at those relative locations, breaking the Discord plugin's ability to process messages after startup.

The `buildMediaUnderstandingRegistryAsync` function omits the `cfg` argument when calling the sync `buildMediaUnderstandingRegistry`, causing user-configured media providers and their settings to be silently dropped when callers use the async API.

The return type was updated to include `"sandbox"` as a valid host, but the underlying parsing logic was not updated to recognize it, causing sandbox approval-pending results to be silently dropped and embedded channels to lose structured approval prompt metadata.

The negated CLI flag `--no-device-identity` is read using the wrong property name `opts.noDeviceIdentity` instead of `opts.deviceIdentity`, causing the flag to always evaluate to `false` and making the token-only auth path unreachable.

When a slow client is disconnected for exceeding MAX_BUFFERED_BYTES, the per-identity sequence number is not advanced before closing, causing reconnected clients to resume from a stale seq and silently skip gap detection for all missed events.

In the timed-out `/new` recovery branch, the fallback logic searches for the newest `*.jsonl.reset.*` file across the entire `workspace/sessions` directory without scoping the lookup to the current session, meaning a more recently reset unrelated conversation could be copied into the wrong memory note, causing cross-conversation data corruption or leakage in multi-chat workspaces.

Replacing the synthesized `search_results` payload with `buildKimiToolMessageContent(toolCall)` causes the tool reply to send back only the raw search query instead of the actual retrieved URLs and content, so Kimi loses the evidence needed to generate a final answer and citations.

When a mid-stream failure triggers a retry of the `/api/chat` request, `accumulatedContent` and `accumulatedToolCalls` are never reset, causing the successful retry's full response to be appended to the partial failed stream and resulting in duplicated text and tool calls.

The protected-directory guard in `resolveExcludePatterns()` only matches exact directory names and fails to block descendant globs like `**/credentials/**`, allowing the tar filter to silently exclude protected contents while leaving an empty folder in the archive, potentially making the backup unrestorable.

The `buildInboundReplayScopeKey()` function keys replay protection differently depending on whether `messageId` or `associatedMessageGuid` is populated, so if a webhook and its retry arrive with different field shapes for the same edit, they hash to distinct scope keys and replay deduplication is bypassed, causing the same edit to be dispatched twice.

The `restoreStreamChunk()` function fails to handle `thinking_delta` and `thinking_end` streaming events, causing `pf_*` privacy placeholders to leak unrestored into live reasoning output shown to users instead of the original sensitive values.

The postinstall rewrite logic unconditionally computes `nextProgram` from the npm package path, causing `needsRepair` to always return true for repo-backed LaunchAgents and silently repointing the service away from the local dev checkout during a routine global package install.

The `parseMcpPayload` function incorrectly parses SSE responses line-by-line instead of assembling complete events, which can cause it to throw or return the wrong payload when a single event spans multiple `data:` lines or when progress/notification frames follow the actual tool result.

When a trusted proxy sends requests without forwarding headers, the new fallback to returning the raw socket address causes proxied traffic to be misidentified as local direct traffic, bypassing local-only authentication gates like Canvas access that should fail closed in misconfigured proxy environments.

The awareness session entry is written to the store keyed by `params.agentId` instead of the agent derived from `sessionKey`, causing cross-agent cron runs to silently drop awareness when `chat.inject` looks in the wrong store and returns "session not found".

Returning `null` for `"overloaded"` in `resolveAuthProfileFailureReason` prevents `markAuthProfileFailure()` from persisting cooldown state, causing `runWithModelFallback` to bypass the cooldown-aware rotation logic and repeatedly hammer an overloaded provider instead of failing over, which worsens latency and request pressure during sustained outages.

Classifying all single-label (bare-name) hosts as private causes `isPrivateOrLoopbackHost` to return `true` for hostnames like "synapse," which skips the `allowPrivateNetwork` opt-in prompt during onboarding but still triggers rejection later in `assertHttpUrlTargetsPrivateNetwork()`, breaking Docker/Kubernetes deployments by creating a misleading gap between onboarding validation and actual connection failure.

The duplicate-registration branch uses a shared numeric refcount per key instead of tracking individual adapter owners, so when one of multiple registrants unregisters, the map may retain a stale adapter instance while ignoring the still-live owner, causing `bind/list/resolve` operations to route through invalid state.

Writing `runningAtMs = undefined` and `lastStatus = "error"` before `applyJobResult` completes creates a race window where a process crash can leave a timed-out job in a state that bypasses backoff logic and allows it to be immediately re-queued or re-executed on restart, skipping intended delay or disable behavior.

Since the `[http_service]` block was removed from `fly.toml`, the gateway is solely exposed via Tailscale, but the deploy workflow still treats `TAILSCALE_AUTHKEY` as optional, meaning a deployment without that secret will succeed yet leave the gateway completely unreachable.

`allowedRoots` is unconditionally passed to `createApplyPatchTool()` regardless of whether `tools.fs.workspaceOnly` is enabled, meaning setting `tools.fs.allowedRoots` alone can silently grant `apply_patch` write access to additional directories outside the intended workspace guard.
`OPENAI_CODEX_DEFAULT_MODEL` is imported twice from the same module in `openai-codex-provider.ts`, causing a TypeScript duplicate identifier error that breaks the build.

The new `sourceProvider` and sender identity fields added to `PluginHookAgentContext` are never populated when `runBeforeReset()` is called in `commands-core.ts`, meaning trust-aware plugin policies are silently bypassed for user-triggered reset commands.

Because the per-store lock is released before the network refresh completes, `writeCredentialToAgentStore()` can overwrite a newer, legitimately advanced OAuth credential with a stale token pair when a concurrent writer updates the profile in the interim.

The import path for the bundled web-search registry was incorrectly changed from `../bundled-web-search-registry.js` to `./bundled-web-search-registry.js`, pointing to the wrong directory and causing a module-not-found error at load time.

When a command is invoked with no arguments, mapping it to `args: null` instead of `args: []` causes `addAllowlistEntry` to skip setting `matchMode: "exact"`, creating a path-only allowlist rule that incorrectly permits any future invocation of that command—including malicious ones with arguments—without prompting.

The mention-extraction regex lacks a trailing token boundary, causing it to greedily match leading digits of longer non-mention tokens (e.g., `@123456789012345:1@lid`), which can accidentally resolve to real WhatsApp participant JIDs and trigger unwanted pings.

When an OAuth provider redirects back with an error parameter (e.g., `?error=access_denied`), the code ignores it and waits for a `code` that will never arrive, causing a 60-second hang before throwing a misleading "Missing authorization code" error instead of immediately surfacing the real OAuth failure.

For shell-wrapper `system.run` requests, invalid or blocked environment variable keys are silently stripped by `sanitizeSystemRunEnvOverrides()` before `sanitizeHostExecEnvWithDiagnostics()` can reject them, causing the new fail-closed hardening to be bypassed and leaving behavior inconsistent with `createExecTool()`.

Adding the push-only `build-artifacts` job as a dependency of `build-smoke` causes `build-smoke` to be silently skipped on pull requests, removing previously enforced build and smoke test coverage for PRs.

The new replay sanitizer incorrectly calls `resolveStructuredAllowedToolName()` after `normalizeToolCallNameForDispatch()`, undoing the fail-closed behavior for ambiguous provider-mangled tool names and allowing replayed tool calls to be rewritten to the wrong concrete tool when multiple candidates exist.

The `startAutoConnect` function silently drops connection attempts if `gatewayAutoReconnectEnabled` is false, but since `disconnectGateway()` sets this flag to false and it isn't restored until after this guard runs in `applyGatewayConnectConfig`, tapping Connect after a Disconnect results in a no-op and the gateway never reconnects.

The output guard is applied only to final payloads after `runEmbeddedAttempt` completes, leaving the messaging tool delivery path unguarded and allowing unsafe content to reach users before any blocking or redaction can occur.

The inbound clone-dedupe key omits reply/quote context, so two messages with identical bodies (e.g., "yes" or "ok") sent within the 15-second deduplication window but referencing different quoted messages will incorrectly collide and cause the second message to be silently dropped as a duplicate.

Chunks arriving within the throttle delay window are accumulated but never emitted as `text_delta` events, causing the `text_end` event to carry no content and potentially dropping entire short replies or the tail of longer ones in `blockReplyBreak: "text_end"` flows.

When `syncPluginRuntimeState` rebinds to the startup registry and cache key, it causes lazily bootstrapped channel plugins to silently disappear because `resolveOutboundChannelPlugin()` memoizes bootstrap attempts by registry key and never re-attempts them, permanently breaking outbound sends to those channels until the process restarts.

The duplicate-ID check in `cron.add` unconditionally calls `.toLowerCase()` on every stored job's `id` without guarding against non-string or missing `id` values that can exist in malformed or legacy-corrupt JSON stores, causing a `TypeError` that breaks all add operations until the store file is manually repaired.

The non-interactive `github-copilot` auth branch fails to set a default model (e.g., `github-copilot/gpt-4o`), causing model resolution to fall back to Anthropic defaults that don't use the Copilot token, resulting in immediate auth failures despite a seemingly successful onboard.

Moving SMS/call-log feature flags into `defaultConfig` removes the `play`/`thirdParty` product flavor variants that existing build scripts, test commands, and release pipelines still depend on, breaking all documented Android build and release workflows.

The `/interrupt` command sends an `agent` RPC to the gateway without the required `idempotencyKey`, causing the request to fail validation and silently drop the re-queued turn while the user incorrectly receives a success message.

The `prettify_json` function accepts an unbounded caller-supplied `indent` value that drives per-line string expansion via `" ".repeat(...)`, enabling a denial-of-service attack through massive memory allocations even on small JSON payloads, and should be clamped to a safe maximum before use.
