The `splitMinimaxToolCalls()` function incorrectly promotes every `<invoke>` block in a message to an executable tool call whenever a `minimax:tool_call` marker is present anywhere, rather than scoping execution only to `<invoke>` blocks nested within valid MiniMax wrapper elements, allowing unintended tool dispatch from quoted or prose examples.

Moving the listener registry to a process-global `Map` causes overlapping instances (e.g., during reload or in tests) to incorrectly delete each other's registrations on shutdown, since the older instance's unconditional `setActiveWebListener(..., null)` will wipe out the newer instance's entry, resuracing "No active WhatsApp Web listener" failures.

When `readAuthStoreMtimeMs` returns `null` (i.e., the auth file is absent), the cache is incorrectly populated with an empty store and all subsequent calls short-circuit before syncing external CLI credentials, leaving auth resolution permanently stale until a process restart.

A stale health completion callback clears the `in_flight` lock before checking its generation, creating a race window where a second concurrent probe can be spawned and its slower result can overwrite fresher health data with stale degraded/running state after restarts or unit switches.

Changing the anonymous-probe check from inspecting individual auth fields to requiring `opts.auth === undefined` causes loopback probes that pass an empty `auth` object (e.g., from unresolved SecretRef paths) to be treated as authenticated, potentially attaching device identity and masking credential-resolution failures in status output.

After a session store is migrated to the `sessions.d` directory format, the subagent depth checker continues reading the old `sessions.json` file, causing spawn depth to always resolve to 1 and allowing over-limit subagents to keep spawning unchecked.

The `operon-guard scan` command always exits with code 0 regardless of findings, so using it as a pre-install gate in scripts will silently pass even when prompt-injection or PII issues are detected, making the safety check ineffective without a documented workaround.

Unresolved Git merge conflict markers remain in `llm-task-tool.ts`, causing invalid TypeScript syntax that will break compilation and prevent the extension from being built or consumed until the conflict is resolved.

Moving `startTtlTimer()` before `await fireStart()` introduces a race condition where a slow first start can allow the TTL to fire `fireStop()` and set `closed = true` mid-await, yet `keepaliveLoop.start()` is still called unconditionally afterward without re-checking `closed`, causing keepalive pings to resume after an explicit auto-stop.

Suppressing `lastExitStatus` and `lastExitReason` fields when a service is in the "running" state at the data layer causes downstream diagnostic flows to lose critical crash exit codes for services that have already auto-restarted back to active, masking real failures; this suppression should instead be applied only in the display formatter.

When ACP inline-delivery spawns a child run without populating `inputProvenance`, the `turnSourceChannel` fallback incorrectly coerces the origin to `webchat` instead of preserving the true channel (e.g. Discord), causing exec approval handlers that check the source channel to reject approvals for those spawned runs entirely.

The updated `parseCameraSnapPayload()` function incorrectly requires a `base64` field, causing a regression where valid URL-only camera payloads from older or plugin nodes fail with an "invalid camera.snap payload" error instead of being handled by the existing `writeUrlToFile()` path.

The new "hard" deny rules in SHIELD.md are not a durable security boundary because the model retains write/edit access to the file itself, allowing it to overwrite the policy and recover blocked tools on the next turn.

Removing the `MOONSHOT_NATIVE_BASE_URLS` and `MODELSTUDIO_NATIVE_BASE_URLS` constants causes a `ReferenceError` at runtime (and a type-check failure) because `applyNativeStreamingUsageCompat` still references them later in the file, breaking provider normalization for Moonshot and Model Studio configurations.

The skip-key derivation uses `resolveStateDir` at install time, which may resolve to a legacy or alias directory that differs from the `~/.openclaw/.env` path the daemon actually reads at runtime, causing injected credentials to be silently dropped after installation.

A wildcard allowlist entry like `"*"` is stripped by `normalizeHostnameAllowlist`, producing an empty array that the updated `matchesHostnameAllowlist` now treats as deny-all instead of allow-all, causing all hostname checks to fail for configs that relied on `*` to disable host filtering.

Removing the `completed` field from the compaction `phase: "end"` event payload breaks multiple downstream listeners that rely on `evt.data.completed === true` to recognize successful compactions, causing attempt counts to stop incrementing and memory-flush dedup to no longer latch.

When smart routing reroutes a model, it silently drops the `localFallback` and `orchestratorFallback` targets from `resolveOrchestratorRoute()`, causing orchestrator outages to abort the run entirely instead of falling back to the local model as intended.

The Azure custom endpoint verification incorrectly probes the `chat/completions` surface instead of the `openai-responses` API that the runtime config uses, causing valid Azure Responses API deployments to be falsely rejected during onboarding.

Incorrectly adding `google-gemini-cli-auth` to `LEGACY_REMOVED_PLUGIN_IDS` will cause any config validation failure for that still-active plugin to be silently swallowed as a stale-config warning instead of surfacing a real error, potentially disabling Gemini CLI authentication without any visible indication.

The new `fallbackOnErrors` field is added to the `AgentModelConfig` type but the corresponding Zod schema remains `.strict()` with only `primary` and `fallbacks` defined, meaning any user who attempts to configure this feature will receive a validation error and be unable to use it.

When `HTTPS_PROXY` is set, `fetchAgent` is incorrectly assigned an undici `EnvHttpProxyAgent` dispatcher instead of a Node `https.Agent`, causing media uploads (images, documents, audio) to fail because Baileys' upload path passes `fetchAgent` directly into `http[s].request({ agent })`, which expects a native Node agent.

The watchdog gates recovery on `health --json` exit status, but because that command only exits non-zero when the gateway is fully unreachable — not when channels are wedged or stale — the watchdog silently no-ops in the exact "process alive but unhealthy" scenarios it was designed to catch.

Because `probeGatewayMemoryStatus()` maps all `callGateway()` failures — including permanent errors like unknown methods or auth failures — to a generic "unavailable" status, the new prefix-only check silently skips verification for `provider: "local"` setups, allowing `openclaw doctor` to falsely report local memory as healthy even when the gateway was never successfully contacted.

The removal of the `shouldRetryTurnWithFreshHandle` recovery path in the catch block means that transient `acpx` process exits no longer trigger a retry with a new session handle, causing users to receive hard `ACP_TURN_FAILED` errors instead of being transparently recovered.

In `persistGeneratedOwnerDisplaySecret`, a three-way async merge using RFC 7396 JSON Merge Patch semantics incorrectly treats keys present in the freshly-read on-disk config but absent from the stale `nextConfig` snapshot as intentional deletions, causing concurrent config additions from other processes to be silently lost during the async write window.

The new "no authorizers configured = unrestricted" fallback was only added to the TypeScript channel implementation but not synced to the mirrored CommonJS shim used by the root SDK export, causing plugins that import via the root entrypoint to incorrectly deny commands when no allowlist is configured.

The `@mariozechner/pi-*` package versions were bumped to `0.58.4` in `package.json` but the `pnpm-lock.yaml` was not regenerated to match, causing CI installs to fail with `ERR_PNPM_OUTDATED_LOCKFILE` due to `--frozen-lockfile` being enabled by default.
The replay loader unconditionally discards persisted queues older than 5 minutes, defeating the purpose of persistence by causing message loss whenever restart downtime exceeds that hardcoded TTL.
Relay targets are matched by display name instead of unique Feishu ID, so any mention of a shared display name can incorrectly trigger message dispatch to the wrong bot account.

The `readDurableStateEnvKeys` function resolves the `.env` path using `resolveStateDir` (which may use legacy dirs or `CLAWDBOT_STATE_DIR`), but the installed daemon loads its dotenv via `resolveConfigDir`, causing auth credentials to be incorrectly skipped at install time and missing at runtime when the two paths diverge.

The socket configuration incorrectly assigns `proxyAgents.wsAgent` to Baileys' `fetchAgent` instead of `proxyAgents.fetchAgent`, causing HTTP-based requests like media and profile fetches to use the wrong proxy agent and potentially fail in proxied WhatsApp setups.

Renaming the `append` parameter to `appendExisting` in `openWritableFileWithinRoot` is a breaking SDK change that causes existing callers passing `append: true` to silently truncate files instead of appending, risking data loss.

When `senderId` is missing, using `directIdParent` (a truncated session key) as the sender fallback instead of the full `directId` can cause sender-scoped policy rules to silently fail to match, potentially granting broader tool access than intended.
The `supabase.saveInstance` RPC method is called by the new profile save flow but is never registered in the gateway, causing every save attempt to fail with an "unknown method" error.

Passing `sessionKey` to `requestHeartbeatNow` breaks global-scope setups by turning unscoped hook wakes into targeted wakes that resolve to the absent `main` agent, causing `hook:wake` and `hook:<jobId>` events to be silently skipped even though they were already enqueued.

Setting `hasAppliedImageModelOverride` on every image turn unconditionally skips `resolveStoredModelOverride()`, causing user-specified or parent-inherited model overrides to be silently ignored in favor of the default `imageModel` whenever an attachment is present.

The `isLikelyVisionModel` heuristic incorrectly flags all model IDs containing "gpt-5" as image-capable, which will cause text-only GPT-5-family models (e.g., `openai-gpt-52`) to advertise image support and send `input_image` payloads that the upstream model cannot handle.

`isActiveThreadBindingRecord` uses `isConfiguredDiscordThreadBindingAccount` which only checks if a Discord account is enabled rather than verifying `threadBindings.enabled`, causing stale bindings from disabled accounts to be treated as active foreign owners and incorrectly dropping valid thread messages until those bindings expire.

When an agent directory name doesn't round-trip through normalization, the real on-disk path is discarded in favor of the normalized version, causing session listings to surface transcripts that subsequent detail lookups (key, timeseries, logs) cannot open because they reconstruct the wrong file path.

Switching from `transformAzureUrl(baseUrl, modelId)` to `transformAzureConfigUrl(baseUrl)` causes Azure deployment URLs that already contain `/openai/deployments/<deployment>` to have `/openai/v1` incorrectly appended to the full path, persisting a malformed base URL that the runtime cannot call.

When the dynamic model wins the preference check, spreading `discoveredHeaders` after `dynamicHeaders` causes stale headers from `models.json` to override configured provider headers, breaking auth and routing for setups that rely on `Authorization`, `User-Agent`, or tenant-routing header precedence.

The usage normalizer does not map DeepSeek's `prompt_cache_hit_tokens` and `prompt_cache_miss_tokens` fields separately, causing all cached tokens to be incorrectly billed at the higher cache-miss rate and systematically overestimating costs whenever context caching is used.

The newly implemented MCP manager is never wired into the runtime tool list returned by `createOpenClawTools`, making MCP tools and resource context completely unreachable in production even when `agents.*.mcp.servers` is configured.

The new `hookContext` passes the raw `chatId` instead of Feishu's canonical `feishuTo` identifier (e.g., `user:${senderOpenId}` for DMs, `chat:${chatId}` for groups), causing `message_sent` hook events to emit a mismatched `conversationId` that won't correlate with preceding `message_received`/`inbound_claim` events, breaking any plugin that relies on conversation-level event matching.

When `jobs.json` is reloaded from a stale snapshot, an outdated numeric `runningAtMs` marker is not overwritten in memory, causing the stuck-job cleanup logic to falsely mark an active job as idle and potentially launch a duplicate concurrent execution.

Recalled memories are injected into prompts without truncation, so long persisted messages can add thousands of tokens to every subsequent prompt, inflating cost and potentially overflowing the model's context window.

Delivery routing fields (`lastChannel`, `lastTo`, `lastThreadId`, `deliveryContext`) are incorrectly cleared on every fresh non-isolated cron/webhook session because the assignments were moved into the wrong code path (`isNewSession === false`), breaking downstream route inference for follow-up messages.

The `bluebubbles` platform is missing from the `VALID_PLATFORMS` allowlist in `extractPlatformFromSessionKey`, causing BlueBubbles sessions to fall back to unprefixed sender IDs that can collide with other channels and leak memory across users.

`hasAnthropicVertexAvailableAuth()` incorrectly returns `true` in metadata-server environments (e.g., Cloud Run, App Engine) even when no project ID can be resolved, causing the `anthropic-vertex` provider to advertise itself as available and then fail at request time when constructing the Vertex client without a required project ID.

The `deliverMediaGroupReply` function buffers all album attachments in memory simultaneously before making any API calls, which can cause severe memory spikes and potential OOM crashes compared to the previous sequential load/send approach, especially when handling multiple large video files that Telegram supports up to 100MB each.

When a stored API key references an environment variable marker, the code resolves it to a plaintext secret before persisting it via `setCredential()`, silently overwriting the env-backed configuration with an inline secret and breaking future environment-variable-based key rotation.

The silent-reply suppression logic is only applied inside `finalizeTextDelivery` but is bypassed for threaded delivery targets (e.g., Telegram forum topics), causing the literal "NO_REPLY" token to leak to end users on those code paths.

The fallback authorization logic incorrectly requires both `provider` and `model` fields together, causing trusted fallback plugins that pass only `model` (a valid partial override) to hard-fail before dispatch, breaking previously supported behavior that allows independent `provider` or `model` overrides.

The incident is resolved using the wrong key (`${channelId}:${accountId}`) before the new incident is even recorded (which uses `${type}:${channel}`), causing successfully recovered channel crashes to remain permanently marked as active incidents and potentially triggering spurious OAG analysis or rollbacks.

The audio branch condition only matches the exact string `audio/ogg` but the outbound flow rewrites the MIME type to `audio/ogg; codecs=opus`, causing voice note messages to fall through to the document payload branch and be rendered as file attachments instead of voice notes.

Calling `resolveMatrixInboundRoute()` before the mention gate causes unmentioned or unauthorized messages to inadvertently update `lastActivityAt` on matched bindings, which can prevent ACP/session bindings from expiring even when no message is actually routed to the agent.

The `authHeader` fix is only applied to the explicit Anthropic compatibility branch, leaving the "Unknown (detect automatically)" probe path still calling `requestAnthropicVerification` without `authHeader`, which means Bearer-only Anthropic-compatible endpoints will continue to fail auto-detection and trap users in a retry loop.

The `resolveSandboxedBridgeMediaPath()` function only allowlists `params.sandbox.allowedRoots` without including the standard trusted media directories resolved by `resolveMediaToolLocalRoots()`, causing valid attachments and generated files stored under default media roots to be incorrectly rejected in sandbox mode.

The blind-create fallback is triggered by any `listMattermostCommands` error (timeouts, 5xx, etc.) rather than only 403 permission-denied responses, which can cause transient failures to incorrectly force create-only mode and leave slash callbacks inactive on deployments with existing commands.

The `parseXmlParameterValue()` function incorrectly coerces all digit-only string parameters to numbers, breaking tools that expect string identifiers like message routing IDs, Discord snowflakes, and phone numbers, which can cause precision loss or failed/misdirected tool calls.

The `resolveTeamGroupId` function unnecessarily calls `GET /teams/{id}` before fetching messages, requiring extra `Team.ReadBasic.All` permissions that aren't needed for channel message reads, causing silent failures and the feature never activating in tenants that only grant `ChannelMessage.Read.All`.

The LSP `initialize` request sends `rootUri: null` instead of the session's `workspaceDir`, causing project-aware language servers like tsserver, pyright, and rust-analyzer to start without a workspace root, which will result in incomplete or broken hover, definition, and references for files in the repo.

The new `usage` lifecycle phase branch cleans up `agentRunSeq` entries but never calls `clearAgentRunContext`, causing a permanent memory leak in the gateway's run-context map for every late-arriving `phase: "usage"` event.

The durable state skip list is built from key names alone rather than filtering to non-empty values, so empty placeholder entries in the `.env` file (e.g., `OPENAI_API_KEY=`) will incorrectly suppress valid auth-ref secrets from being copied into the service environment, causing secret resolution failures at runtime.

Re-running the custom onboard flow unconditionally overwrites a user's intentional text-only input configuration with `["text", "image"]` based on model ID heuristics, which can cause unsupported image payloads to be sent to proxies that only accept text.

The `sanitizeProfileId` function fails to strip trailing bare ESC bytes (`\x1b`) in profile IDs because the regex requires a character after `\x1b`, leaving a terminal injection vulnerability when unsanitized profile IDs are printed in terminal output.

The new `enqueue` and `abort` proxy wrappers added to `resolvePluginRuntime` expose methods that don't exist on the underlying gateway subagent runtime, meaning any plugin that calls them will crash with a `TypeError` at runtime.

Using `withCheckedContinuation` for the contacts permission request ignores Swift task cancellation, meaning that when an invoke times out, the suspended task will remain blocked until the user dismisses the OS prompt, allowing stuck tasks to accumulate and degrade node responsiveness.

The new lean daemon status branch emits synthetic `installed: null` / `loadedText: "unknown"` service entries when config is missing, replacing previously unconditional real daemon state reads and causing `status --json` to falsely report an unknown service state on fresh installs or reset machines that scripts and UIs rely on to decide whether to install or start the service.

Document batching logic incorrectly groups unrelated Telegram channel posts within a 1.5-second window by collapsing them to the same synthetic sender ID, causing distinct messages to be merged into a single agent turn with no way to recover the original message boundaries.

The `sms.search` command is only declared to the gateway when `READ_SMS` is already granted, so on a fresh install the gateway rejects the command before dispatch ever reaches the new permission-request flow, leaving the intended first-use `READ_SMS` prompt permanently unreachable.

The `extractRemoteConfigGetPayload` function fails to unwrap the nested config snapshot returned by `config.get`, causing `resolveNodeCommandAllowlist()` to receive the entire snapshot object instead of the actual config, so `cfg.gateway?.nodes` is always undefined and command allow/deny lists silently fall back to platform defaults in remote live-test runs.

`shouldUseAnthropicAuthorizationHeader()` only checks user-supplied provider configs and ignores implicit providers injected by `resolveImplicitProviders()`, so built-in Anthropic-compatible providers like `minimax` will never have the required `Authorization: Bearer` header applied.

The `isFromFallback` flag is never cleared by some runtime-model writers, causing the new guard to silently skip valid model assignments in those code paths and fall back to incorrect defaults for all subsequent requests in that session.

The Argus recovery retry relies solely on an in-process `setTimeout`, so if the gateway restarts during the delay window, the persisted `recoveryResumeAt` metadata is never scanned or acted upon, leaving the session stuck in `recoveryState: "active"` and silently dropping the promised automatic retry.

The workspace override is only applied to the run environment but not to memory operations, causing `memory_search`/`memory_get` to still resolve against the agent's default workspace directory, which breaks context isolation and can leak notes from the wrong project.

When normalizing Gemini usage, `promptTokenCount` (which includes cached tokens) is mapped entirely to `input` without extracting `cachedContentTokenCount` into `cacheRead`, causing `estimateUsageCost()` to price cached tokens at the full input rate and report zero cache hits for any request using Gemini context caching.

The new `mirrorWebchatToLastExternalChannel` mirroring logic is placed inside an `if (!agentRunStarted)` guard, so it is never triggered for the most common case where a user message actually launches the AI agent, leaving agent-driven webchat replies unmirrored.

Because document messages are buffered for up to 1.5 seconds while subsequent non-document messages (e.g., text commands) are enqueued immediately, a follow-up instruction can reach the agent before the buffered file attachments, reversing message order and causing the agent to process commands without the intended documents.

The preview path emits `emptyAllowlistWarnings` without sanitization, allowing crafted config keys containing control characters or ANSI escapes to inject forged content into terminal output, breaking the existing log sanitization guardrail.

Treating grammY "Network request failed after N attempts" errors as always safe to retry in `isSafeToRetrySendError` risks duplicate message delivery, because these envelopes can wrap ambiguous post-write transport failures where Telegram may have already processed the original send.

`resolveWorkspaceBoundary` widens the trust boundary to the default workspace root rather than the user-configured workspace directory, allowing bootstrap files (e.g., SOUL.md, AGENTS.md) to be silently imported from sibling or parent workspace directories outside the intended isolation scope.

Third-party bundles can exploit the new SSE transport branch to trigger arbitrary outbound HTTP connections simply by including a `{ "url": ... }` entry in their `.mcp.json`, bypassing the existing stdio-only trust boundary without requiring any explicit owner opt-in.

RichTextBlock OpenUrl runs write unvalidated URLs directly into anchor `href` attributes, allowing disallowed protocols to be reached via non-primary navigations (e.g., middle-click) that bypass the `openExternalUrlSafe` guard.

Changing the default value of `supportsUsageInStreaming` from `false` to `true` in `normalizeModelCompat()` bypasses the intentional provider-level gating in `applyNativeStreamingUsageCompat()`, causing non-native Moonshot/ModelStudio proxy endpoints to incorrectly receive `stream_options.include_usage` and potentially break requests on proxy backends that don't support it.

The `resolveExaApiKey` helper only handles literal API key strings and ignores SecretRef objects (e.g., `{ source: "env", ... }`), causing configured Exa API keys to be treated as missing and triggering a `missing_exa_api_key` error instead of resolving the reference.

The new `authHeader` parameter is only passed to `requestAnthropicVerification` in the explicit Anthropic compatibility branch, but the automatic unknown-endpoint detection path omits it, causing previously configured Bearer-auth Anthropic-compatible endpoints to fail auto-detection and incorrectly re-enter the retry flow.

Removing the default media extraction path for silent tools breaks automatic delivery of voice and image attachments, because tools like `tts` and `image_generate` rely on tool-result `MEDIA:`/`details.path` data being converted into `payload.mediaUrls` when summaries are off, causing successful calls to produce no outbound message.

The `rust_url_encode` function calls `nativeAddon.urlEncode`, which does not exist as an export in the Rust addon, causing every invocation to fail at runtime with a `TypeError` due to the missing function.

When the same environment variable key exists in both `sandboxEnv` and `skillEnv`, the intended skill-specific value is silently overwritten by the sandbox default because `sandboxEnv` is merged after `skillEnv` in `buildSandboxEnv()`, causing sandboxed execution to diverge from gateway execution and potentially use the wrong credential.

The active-branch pruning loop incorrectly adds shared ancestor messages to `removedIds`, causing `rebuildEntriesAfterRemoval()` to reparent sibling branches to `null` and silently corrupt their history for any branched session with transcript pruning enabled.

The new unconditional catch-all in `formatAssistantErrorText` swallows recognizable plain-text errors (auth failures, model-not-found, session expiry) and replaces their actionable messages with a generic "Something went wrong," leaving users unable to take the correct recovery action.

`waitForIdle()` only awaits a one-time snapshot of `this.tails`, so any tasks enqueued after that snapshot is taken can be silently skipped, causing the method to falsely report idle and allowing teardown/cleanup to proceed while jobs are still pending.

The transcript matching logic in `rehydrateSessionStoreEntries` relies solely on a 5-minute timestamp window to identify transcripts, allowing an unrelated `.jsonl` file to be incorrectly bound to a missing child session key, and because this mapping is persisted to `sessions.json`, a wrong session association can cause replay and continuation against entirely unrelated conversation state.

Because `readUsageFromSessionLog()` unconditionally marks all transcript snapshots as fresh, the new `logUsage.totalTokensFresh` condition always fires, causing `/status` to overwrite a higher preserved `totalTokensEstimate` with a smaller, incomplete transcript token count and reintroducing the under-reporting the change was meant to fix.

The timeout logic only rejects the wrapper promise without canceling the underlying `params.op()` task, allowing the queue to advance and start the next session task while the previous one is still running, which can cause concurrent mutations and state corruption for the same ACP session.

The macOS normalization path drops the newly added `args` and `matchMode` fields when rebuilding `ExecAllowlistEntry`, causing "always allow" entries with argument restrictions to be silently downgraded to path-only approvals after a restart or reload cycle.

The `sessions_manage` tool incorrectly forwards an `instructions` field to `sessions.compact`, which rejects it at runtime because the gateway's `SessionsCompactParams` schema only permits `key` and `maxLines` with `additionalProperties: false`, breaking the guided-compaction path entirely.

The documentation now advertises gcloud ADC as a valid auth path for `google-vertex`, but the underlying resolver in `model-auth.ts` still treats a falsy/empty API key as missing auth, meaning users who follow the documented ADC-only setup will hit a broken auth check at runtime.

The fix only addresses directive-only requests, but the normal reply path in `get-reply-run.ts` still overwrites a persisted `xhigh` thinking level down to `high` on non-directive messages, meaning session state will still be corrupted in the common targeted cases.

In `splitMinimaxToolCalls()`, when MiniMax wraps multiple `<invoke>` elements inside a single `<minimax:tool_call>` block, only the first invocation is captured and processed because the regex matches the entire wrapper once and advances the cursor past it, silently dropping all subsequent tool calls in that wrapper.

The reasoning/image heuristic incorrectly matches the "35" in Azure `gpt-35-turbo` deployment names, causing `applyCustomApiConfig()` to set `reasoning: true` and image input capabilities for GPT-3.5 deployments, which will break those setups by sending unsupported image and thinking settings to the API.

A new guard that throws when a provider lacks a `describeImage` handler inadvertently removes the existing fallback path for standard image model providers (e.g., OpenAI/Google) not registered in the provider registry, causing image understanding to fail with an error instead of using the built-in model-based describer.

Gating `threadRootId` on `replyToMode !== "off"` unintentionally corrupts inbound session identity, causing messages from distinct Mattermost threads to collapse into a single session and breaking thread-aware flows that rely on `MessageThreadId`, `ReplyToId`, and related context fields.

Appending implicit runtime entries causes the WhatsApp `runtime-api.ts` to be included in the default release bundle, but because `extensions/whatsapp/package.json` does not set `openclaw.bundle.stageRuntimeDependencies`, its required dependencies (`@whiskeysockets/baileys`, `jimp`) are never vendored, meaning bundled installs will throw module-not-found errors at runtime when the WhatsApp runtime is accessed.

During a forced reload triggered by an external edit to `jobs.json`, a missing `try/catch` around `computeJobNextRunAtMs()` means an invalid cron expression or timezone will throw an unhandled exception, causing the entire reload to fail and halting processing of all unrelated cron jobs until the file is corrected.

When a named account explicitly sets a token field to an empty string, the new base-credential gate incorrectly suppresses the `default` account ID, causing `listAccountIds()` to omit the valid base account and preventing its gateway from starting at runtime.

The `usedEstimate` flag is incorrectly derived from the presence of `totalTokensEstimate` rather than the winning branch of `resolveGatewaySessionTotalTokens()`, causing `totalTokensFresh` to be set to `false` even when the fresh transcript total wins, which leads downstream consumers like ACP to drop valid usage snapshots and lose the current context size.

When `preferWorkspaceEntries` is true, the code bypasses the stored skill snapshot (which was filtered by `resolveAgentSkillsFilter`) and reloads the entire workspace skill catalog, causing sandboxed agents that were intentionally restricted to a subset of skills to advertise and provision environment variables for all eligible workspace skills instead.

Replacing `session.channel`/`session.chatType` with `origin.*` fields for bridged sessions causes per-session rows to incorrectly reflect the originating channel (e.g., webchat/direct) instead of the actual delivery channel (e.g., telegram/group), breaking UI badges, filters, queries, and CSV exports that rely on those row fields and do not fall back to `origin`.

When `GITHUB_TOKEN` is set, the unquoted `$GITHUB_AUTH` variable expands into multiple shell tokens, causing `curl` to receive the `-H` flag, the header name, and the token value as separate arguments rather than a single argument, which breaks authenticated GitHub API requests.

Returning `null` when `models.copilotDiscovery.enabled` is `false` inadvertently removes the synthesized Copilot provider for profile-backed authentication setups, causing model IDs like `github-copilot/gpt-4o` to lose their provider backing and fail, because the toggle should only skip the startup token discovery exchange rather than disabling all Copilot usage for existing auth profiles.

The supplementary usage event is emitted after `runEmbeddedPiAgent` has already fired its terminal lifecycle `end`/`error` event, which causes `createAgentEventHandler` to tear down the chat link and run context before the `phase: "usage"` event arrives, resulting in the usage payload being silently dropped for queued follow-up runs.

The pathological-input freeze guard only counts code fences anchored at column 0, missing valid Markdown fences with up to three leading spaces, so the exponential-backtracking hang in `marked.parse()` can still be triggered by inputs containing many unclosed indented fences.

Adding a `maximum: 3600` constraint to `runTimeoutSeconds` in the schema contradicts the runtime's documented support for arbitrary non-negative values (where `0` means no timeout), causing legitimate long-running subagent jobs exceeding one hour to fail schema validation and become impossible to spawn.

Computing `isFromFallback` by comparing requested model/provider values instead of the actually-executed model from `result.meta.agentMeta` can incorrectly pass `false` in CLI resume sessions, bypassing the safeguard in `updateSessionStoreAfterAgentRun` and permanently pinning the session to a fallback model instead of the configured primary.

The new relative import path `../../../src/channels/plugins/config-schema.js` in the Nostr plugin ties the runtime file to the monorepo source tree, causing a missing module error at startup in packaged installs where no top-level `src/` directory exists.

The `isSafeToRetrySendError` function incorrectly returns `true` for grammY's generic "failed after N attempts" error envelope, which can cause duplicate messages by retrying non-idempotent Telegram sends even when the original request may have already been delivered.

When `sessions_spawn(..., thread=true)` is called from a top-level Matrix room with no existing thread, the session incorrectly binds to the entire room instead of creating a dedicated child thread, causing the spawned session to hijack all non-threaded messages in that room.

Including `RECOVERABLE_TOOL_ERROR_KEYWORDS` (which contains "missing") in `SELF_CORRECTABLE_TOOL_ERROR_KEYWORDS` causes hard, unrecoverable mutating tool failures — such as Discord "missing permissions" errors — to be silently suppressed as self-correctable, hiding the failure warning from users even though the action never succeeded.

The `modelOverride` field intended to take priority in `resolveModelSelection` is always `undefined` in practice because `listSessions` payloads strip that metadata before returning rows, so heartbeat and compaction events still incorrectly overwrite the status bar model, leaving the fix ineffective outside of mocked tests.

The `stop()` method permanently deletes all in-memory Feishu thread bindings without persisting them to disk, causing all `/focus` state to be irrecoverably lost on any monitor restart or transient reconnect since there is no reload path to restore them.

If SHIELD removes a client tool that was already pinned via `tool_choice` by the upstream gateway, the model receives a "must call X" prompt with no matching tool definition, causing the request to silently degrade into a plain-text response instead of the required function call.

When a sub-agent refreshes an OAuth token and writes back to the main auth store, it may use a stale in-memory snapshot instead of the current on-disk file, causing unrelated credential or usage updates made after the snapshot was activated to be silently clobbered.

The deduplication key in `buildDirectOutboundDedupeKey` omits session context fields (e.g. `agentId`, session `key`), causing legitimate outbound sends from different sessions with the same target and payload to be incorrectly collapsed within the 1.5s window, which can silently drop sends and misattribute or skip internal hooks.

The private-mode policy validation fails to check the `agents.defaults.compaction.model` override, allowing a config that appears compliant to silently route transcript summarization through a remote provider (e.g., OpenAI) when automatic compaction triggers on long conversations.

When constructing a quoted reply, the code incorrectly builds the `contextInfo.quotedMessage` from the new outgoing message text rather than fetching the original target message, causing every reply to display a quote bubble of itself instead of the intended message being replied to.
The bundle MCP runtime is created before lock acquisition but only disposed in an inner `finally` block, meaning spawned MCP subprocesses will leak if `acquireSessionWriteLock` fails or times out.

The abort cleanup path calls `removeChatRun` using the client/idempotency key instead of the agent `runId` used by `addChatRun`, causing a key mismatch that can strand registry entries and incorrectly remap future agent events to an already-aborted chat run.

A lost-wakeup race condition in `waitForActiveRetryRunsToFinish` can cause `drainPendingDecryptions()` to block forever if the last retry completes after the `activeRetryRuns === 0` check but before the resolver is added to `retryIdleResolvers`, preventing the Matrix monitor from shutting down.

Including `AZURE_OPENAI_ENDPOINT` in `providerAuthEnvVars` causes `resolveEnvApiKey()` to incorrectly treat the endpoint URL as a valid API key, bypassing auth checks and leading to confusing 401/403 failures at runtime.

The process-group cleanup via `killProcessTree()` is inadvertently skipped on successful (non-aborted) turn completions because `childStillRunning` is already false by the time the `finally` block runs, allowing detached grandchild subprocesses to leak in production.

Raw, unescaped skill names from omitted/truncated skills are being concatenated directly into the prompt via `formatTruncatedSkillNames`, creating a prompt-injection vulnerability where malicious skill names could influence model behavior even though those skills were intentionally excluded from the available skills list.

In isolated mode, `migrate_legacy_host_data` is called before the Docker image is built or pulled, causing the setup script to exit fatally on `docker compose run` if the image doesn't yet exist, preventing setup from completing whenever legacy host config requires migration.
The `expectFalJsonPost` helper function was deleted from `fal.test.ts`, but remaining tests still call it, causing an unresolved reference error that breaks the entire fal provider test suite.

When `ensureApiKeyFromOptionEnvOrPrompt` returns a `SecretRef` object instead of a plain string, `buildFoundryProviderConfig` skips the Azure API-key auth wiring (the `authHeader: false` flag and `api-key` header), causing runtime requests to fall back to bearer-style auth and fail with 401/403 errors for ref-backed credentials.

Because `onAssistantMessageStart` is not awaited before `onPartialReply` fires for the next turn, `streamMessageId` may still reference the previous turn's post, causing `schedulePatch()` to overwrite and finalize the wrong Mattermost message with the new turn's content.

When `runHeartbeatOnce` receives a partial heartbeat override (e.g., `{ target: "last" }`) from cron wake-now calls, it silently discards the configured default `preHook`, causing resource and health gate checks to be bypassed entirely.

The MiniMax malformed closing-tag recovery path applies a regex over the entire preceding text block, which can incorrectly match and execute tool calls from quoted examples or other prose, causing unintended tools to be dispatched.

When `dispatchReplyWithBufferedBlockDispatcher` rejects in the inline research callback handler, `spinnerInterval` and `deferTimer` are never cleared in the error path, causing those timers to leak and indefinitely fire repeated Telegram API edit attempts.

The `FsRootSchema` path validation only accepts POSIX-style absolute paths, causing Windows absolute paths (e.g., `C:\packs` or `\\server\share`) to be incorrectly rejected, making the `tools.fs.roots` feature entirely unusable for Windows agents.

When a custom endpoint's saved provider ID collides with a built-in provider namespace (e.g., "openai" or "anthropic"), reusing that ID in `ensureApiKeyFromEnvOrPrompt` causes the built-in provider's credentials to be offered and potentially accepted, which can overwrite the custom endpoint's stored config with the wrong key.

Plugin TTS provider IDs are normalized (e.g., "z.ai" → "zai") before use, but the config lookup uses exact/alias keys, causing API keys and auth settings to go missing and synthesis to fail when users store provider config under an alias key.

Using a positional count (`streamedTurnCount`) to skip final payloads instead of tracking the specific streamed assistant turn can cause the wrong turn to be skipped in multi-tool-call runs, dropping a reply and misaligning subsequent finalizations.

The `GET /api/sessions` HTTP handler silently ignores supported filter parameters (`includeGlobal`, `includeUnknown`, `label`, `spawnedBy`) that the WebSocket path forwards correctly, causing callers that rely on these filters to receive unfiltered or incorrect session results.

The stale active turn recovery logic is non-atomic — a race condition between `readActiveTurn` and `clearActiveTurn` allows a newly written live turn record to be incorrectly deleted, stripping crash-recovery coverage and potentially causing incorrect recovery behavior.

When `preserveErroredAssistantResults` moves a displaced tool result forward via `pushToolResult()`, it fails to set the transcript-changed flag, causing `repairToolUseResultPairing()` to return the original unrepaired message array and leaving providers that enforce strict ordering to reject the still-orphaned tool result.

The hard-coded 62.5s disconnect retry window in `deliverWebReply()` ignores the configurable reconnect backoff from `resolveReconnectPolicy()`, meaning reply retries can be exhausted and throw errors while `monitorWebChannel()` is still sleeping between reconnect attempts, causing message loss whenever operators tune reconnect delays beyond that fixed threshold.

When `current` is a bare alias, the select option value is rewritten to the full `modelId` but the bound `<select>` value in `agents-panels-overview.ts` still uses the raw alias, causing a mismatch where no option is selected and users may unknowingly overwrite their current model.

The new `shouldCompareDynamicOverride` guard incorrectly routes explicitly configured model rows (e.g., an inline `gpt-5.4` entry with a capped `contextWindow`) through the dynamic override path, causing `preferResolvedModel()` to select the synthetic high-context model and allowing downstream prompt budgeting to exceed the backend's real context window.

During gateway hot reload, `stopChannel` unconditionally sets the `manuallyStopped` flag, and if the subsequent `startChannel` call throws before clearing it, the channel becomes permanently stuck in a manually-stopped state that blocks health monitoring and auto-recovery until the gateway is restarted.

The gateway service staleness check incorrectly flags services with custom NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS paths (e.g., corporate CA bundles) as needing a refresh, causing unnecessary reinstalls that drop the custom CA bundle and can break TLS connections.

When an overflow retry injects AGENTS context via `extraSystemPrompt` but the turn still ends in a timeout or error, the `postCompactionContextInjected` flag on the returned `agentMeta` incorrectly suppresses the normal `enqueueSystemEvent()` refresh for the *next* turn, leaving the subsequent user message running against a compacted transcript without Session Startup or Red Lines having been re-read.

If a Telegram polling session gets stuck before completing its first successful `getUpdates` call, the channel health snapshot remains in a state (`connected: undefined`, `lastEventAt: null`) that bypasses the stale-socket check entirely, causing `evaluateChannelHealth()` to incorrectly report healthy even after the grace period expires and preventing the liveness monitor from restarting the hung session.

Batched document processing calls `processMessage()` directly instead of routing through `inboundDebouncer.enqueue()`, causing downstream exceptions to be silently swallowed rather than surfaced to the user as error replies in Telegram.

Setting PAT expiry to `Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER` causes Node.js timer overflow, collapsing the refresh delay to ~1ms and creating a near-tight token refresh loop that wastes CPU and repeatedly writes auth state throughout an agent run.

The onboarding UI now advertises SMS search functionality and treats READ_SMS as granted in its toggle logic, but the onboarding flow never actually requests the READ_SMS permission, leaving users who complete onboarding believing SMS search is enabled when it remains unavailable until they separately grant the permission elsewhere.

A missing import for `loadConfig` causes the function to always throw and return `undefined`, silently preventing `taglineScriptFile` from ever being read from config and causing the "script" tagline mode to render empty for users.

When both native Anthropic and Anthropic Vertex profiles are configured, `resolveAnthropicDefaultAuthMode()` incorrectly returns the auth mode of whichever profile appears first in `auth.order.anthropic`, causing Vertex sessions to inherit OAuth-based pruning defaults and silently lose the cache-retention and heartbeat behavior intended for API-key/ADC semantics.

The mail button callbacks (e.g., `mb:archive:<threadId>`) are never routed to a real handler, so clicking Archive, Reply, or Delete on Telegram silently falls through to the generic message path and sends the raw callback string as a text message instead of performing the intended Gmail action.

Using `Reflect.get(target, property, receiver)` in the read proxy binds getter `this` to the proxy instead of the original engine instance, causing `TypeError` crashes when accessing private class fields (e.g., `#private`) through properties like `info` after `resolveContextEngine` wraps the object.

The sender-session hint is incorrectly shown to the requester agent on its own turn, causing it to target its own session key and potentially stall or infinitely recurse instead of replying to the other agent — the hint should only appear on target turns.

The Matrix subagent hooks are registered via a fire-and-forget dynamic `import()`, creating a race condition window after gateway start or plugin reload where `sessions_spawn(..., thread:true)` calls can fail nondeterministically because the plugin loader doesn't await registration and the subagent spawner immediately rejects requests when no hook is present.

The `isSameOAuthIdentity` function incorrectly treats credentials with a missing `email` field as matching, allowing cross-agent token write-back to silently overwrite the wrong OAuth account when providers don't populate email.

The `parseXmlParameterValue()` function unconditionally trims all parameter values, causing whitespace-sensitive tool arguments (e.g., indented code blocks or text with leading/trailing newlines) to be silently corrupted before being forwarded to tools like `runMessageAction()`.

The genome validation commands load schema files from a `schemas/` directory at runtime, but since `schemas/` is excluded from the npm package's published file list, the commands will crash with an `ENOENT` error when run from an installed CLI rather than a source checkout.

Removing `senderLabel` from the grouping key causes consecutive messages from different users to be merged into a single `MessageGroup` and incorrectly attributed to "You", misidentifying who sent what in shared or imported chats.

The timestamp fence that guards against stale events is never seeded for the `agent` method, so delayed events from a previous run with a reused idempotency key can appear fresh and corrupt chat state before the new run begins.

When building the synthetic transcript message for `chat.send` requests with attachments, image data from `parsedImages` is omitted and only the text from `parsedMessage` is serialized, causing image-only turns to be broadcast as empty messages and mixed text+image turns to lose their attachments for live subscribers until a full refresh.

Unlike `subagent.run()`, which rejects unauthorized model override requests, `subagent.enqueue()` silently strips the `provider`/`model` params and dispatches the job anyway, creating an inconsistent auth enforcement gap that can queue runs against an unintended default model without surfacing an error.

The `updated-message` noise gate incorrectly discards media-only events with empty text by routing them to the `return true` (drop) path, causing inbound photo and sticker attachments delivered via BlueBubbles' `updated-message` webhook to never reach `processMessage`.

The `prunePollParamsForNonPollAction` guard only exempts the `"poll"` action but not `"poll-vote"`, causing required fields like `pollId` and vote option identifiers to be deleted before plugin dispatch and breaking real poll-vote flows.

The Grok provider declares a new `credentialPath` pointing to `plugins.entries.xai.config.webSearch.apiKey`, but its runtime credential accessors still read/write the legacy `tools.web.search.grok.apiKey` path, causing callers that rely on `getConfiguredCredentialValue` to incorrectly treat a properly configured user as unconfigured and skip Grok during auto-detection.

Changing the SMS permission check from AND to OR causes onboarding to incorrectly mark SMS as fully granted when only one of the two required permissions (SEND_SMS or READ_SMS) has been granted, allowing users to skip the flow without ever requesting the missing permission.

The `/session idle` and `/session max-age` commands resolve the wrong Feishu binding for `group_topic_sender` conversations by matching the shared `chat:topic:root` record before the sender-scoped `:sender:<openId>` binding, causing lifecycle changes to be applied to the wrong session while live message routing correctly uses the sender-scoped one.

When only `OPENAI_API_KEY` is set, the provider availability logic incorrectly recognizes only `openai-codex` and not `openai`, causing the startup script to silently overwrite intentionally pinned `openai` models with `openai-codex` defaults in live deployments.

The new cross-agent global session bypass logic is unreachable because `parseAgentSessionKey` always returns `null` for the `"global"`/`"unknown"` keys that `classifySessionKey` produces, causing `sameAgentScope` to always evaluate as `true` and leaving cross-agent global sessions permanently hidden in the dropdown.

The `resolveLlmLogFile` function calls `loadConfig()`, which throws on invalid configurations, causing `openclaw logs` to fail for `--source llm` and `--source all` precisely when users need log access most — during troubleshooting after a broken config edit.

The fuzzy path resolution fallback incorrectly initializes the filesystem walk with an empty root on POSIX systems because splitting an absolute path like "/tmp/foo" on `path.sep` yields an empty first segment, causing the walk to resolve against `process.cwd()` instead of the filesystem root and leaving normalized QMD paths unresolvable on Linux/macOS.

A control-flow bug in `runConfigureWizard()` causes `openclaw configure --sections memory` to either skip Cortex/memory setup entirely or accidentally overwrite `gateway.mode` to `local` on a remote deployment, because section-scoped runs still execute the global gateway prompt and its early-return side effects before reaching the memory branch.

The new `timeoutMs` fetch aborts surface as `AbortError` in Node/undici rather than timeout-named errors, causing the retry logic in `runBatchWithTimeoutRetry` to skip retries and incorrectly treat slow-provider timeouts as ordinary batch failures, which will prematurely disable batch mode.

`stripImageContentForPersistence` eagerly removes toolResult image blocks at write time before they are guaranteed to have been consumed by an assistant turn, meaning that if a run is interrupted mid-session, resuming from persisted JSONL will be missing critical tool output images and the model cannot recover them.

The `ScanControllers` method is missing from `ALLOWED_BRIDGE_METHODS`, causing `invokeBridgeSequence("ScanControllers", ...)` to always be rejected, which prevents virtual controller detection and allows a "real" connection to silently proceed against a virtual target.

Re-exporting Node.js-dependent server-only helpers (pulling in `node:path` and `node:fs`) from the root `plugin-sdk` barrel breaks browser, worker, and jsdom environments for any plugin that imports from the root entrypoint, even though dedicated subpath entrypoints already exist for these APIs.

The new `buildToolContext` always injects the current Telegram topic as `currentThreadTs` based solely on chat ID matching, causing cross-topic tool replies that explicitly target a different topic in the same forum chat to have their intended topic overridden and the message silently delivered to the wrong thread.

Changing the usage predicate from `> 0` to `>= 0` causes `hasNonzeroUsage` to match all-zero delivery-mirror footer entries, which can trick transcript-derived memory-flush logic into reading zeroed token counts and incorrectly skipping context compaction even when the model's context window is near its limit.

The new `retry_after` branch correctly marks 429 rate-limit errors as retryable, but because the retry runner caps and jitters delays to a 30-second maximum, retries can fire before Telegram's directed backoff window expires, causing repeated 429s and potentially exhausting all retry attempts.

The `tryGatewayCall` fallback indiscriminately replaces all errors—including auth failures, disconnections, and server exceptions—with fake demo data, which can silently mislead operators into trusting fabricated diagnostics instead of seeing that a real console request has failed.

The `normalizeModelCompat()` path unconditionally sets `supportsStrictMode = false` for all non-native OpenAI base URLs before `hasOpenRouterStrictToolSupport()` exceptions are ever evaluated, causing `tools[].function.strict` to be stripped from OpenRouter and Anthropic structured-output requests that should legitimately support it.

When a custom `OPENCLAW_TASK_SCRIPT` outside the state directory omits port configuration, `resolveTaskConfigEnvironment` incorrectly rewrites the config root to `dirname(sourcePath)`, causing `resolveConfiguredGatewayPort()` to read the wrong `openclaw.json` and potentially leading `stopScheduledTask()` and `restartScheduledTask()` to probe or kill the wrong listener.

Applying `stripImageContentForPersistence` during persistence transformation immediately removes image content from the latest user turn before an assistant response is received, which breaks retry/resume scenarios by preventing the model from re-reading the original image input.

The installer script hardcodes a different repository (DaevMithran/openclaw-vm) as its clone target, meaning any fixes or updates merged into the current repository (openclaw/openclaw) will never reach end users who follow the documented curl-based install path.

The `compact` action in `sessions-manage-tool.ts` incorrectly calls the `sessions.compact` gateway RPC (which only trims transcript lines) instead of the `/compact` runtime compaction flow, meaning it silently discards transcript data rather than performing the documented summarization-aware semantic compaction.

When the recovery deadline is exceeded before entries are processed, the MAX_RETRIES check is bypassed, causing entries to accumulate ever-increasing retry counts indefinitely without ever being moved to the failed state as intended.

The SSRF policy is hardcoded to `dangerouslyAllowPrivateNetwork: false` rather than being threaded through from user configuration, meaning any SSRF settings defined in `openclaw.json` or `SandboxBrowserSettings` will never reach sandboxed browser navigations.

The Exa plugin is registered via the plugin SDK but never actually reachable in production because the live `web_search` tool path in `web-search.ts` only resolves a hard-coded set of providers (brave, perplexity, grok, gemini, kimi) and does not include Exa, meaning the feature silently never activates even when `EXA_API_KEY` is set.

Calling `routeLogsToStderr()` when `--json` mode is detected redirects all subsequent `console.log`/`runtime.log` output to stderr, which causes JSON commands like `channels list --json` to emit their machine-readable payload on stderr instead of stdout, breaking any caller that parses stdout.

Aborting the shared fetch signal during an outbound restart will cause any in-progress Telegram API calls (e.g., `sendMessage`, `editMessageText`) to fail with an unretryable abort error, silently dropping replies even when the network has recovered.

Broadening the `api_error` JSON match to ignore the error message text causes `classifyFailoverReason()` to misclassify permanent failures (e.g., invalid API key, quota exhaustion) as retryable timeouts, leading to wasteful profile rotation and silent suppression of the real root cause.

The write-back logic only propagates refreshed OAuth tokens through the main store, so agents sharing a profile that exists only in non-main `auth-profiles.json` files can still race on token refresh and trigger `refresh_token_reused` errors despite the new global lock.

When an agent session's main key alias changes mid-run, abort requests are normalized against the new config value rather than the alias stored at run-start, causing `abortAgentRunById` to silently fail and leaving the active run un-abortable via RPC until it times out or finishes naturally.

Appending a uniqueness suffix to `action_id` breaks existing plugin handlers and agent workflows that match on the previously stable `openclaw:reply_button` action ID, since the raw action ID is forwarded downstream into handler payloads and system events without normalization.

Using `new URL("/search", baseUrl)` discards any existing path prefix in `baseUrl`, so SearXNG deployments hosted under a subpath (e.g., `/searxng`) will receive requests at the wrong endpoint, resulting in 404 failures.

The newly added `modelFallbacks` field is declared in the `AgentCompactionConfig` TypeScript type but is missing from the corresponding strict Zod schema, causing config loading to fail with an unknown-key validation error when users attempt to use the documented option.

The parsed terminal session data discards the tmux socket path, causing subsequent actions (create, attach, list) to silently target the wrong tmux server when agent sessions live on a custom socket, making real sessions disappear from the UI and directing users to attach to the wrong instance.

When a task environment only contains `OPENCLAW_PROFILE` but is missing `OPENCLAW_HOME` (as in pre-patch installations), the early-return branch discards the caller shell's config root variables and falls back to the default home directory, causing `gateway stop`/`restart` to resolve the wrong `openclaw.json` and potentially leave the Windows scheduled task running despite reporting success.

The `maxAnnounceChars` truncation logic appends an extra suffix string after slicing to the character limit, causing the final output to exceed the configured cap and potentially fail delivery on platforms with strict size limits like Telegram.

Remapping legacy `grok-4.20` model IDs to new `beta-latest` aliases during normalization breaks downstream exact-ID lookups in model config resolution, silently dropping per-model overrides and potentially failing to resolve models when dynamic plugin hooks are unavailable.

If a `sessions.list` request is already in-flight when a new session is created, the `loadSessions()` guard drops both refresh attempts and the stale list response can overwrite session state, causing the newly created session to disappear from the dropdown until the next refresh or event.

The new vision branch fails to call the cleanup function returned by `MediaAttachmentCache.getPath()`, causing a temp file leak under `/tmp` for every URL-backed image attachment processed on each vision turn until the process restarts.

The new `shouldSuppressOutboundDelivery` guards prevent stale delta sends but fail to cover the downstream `sendPayloadAsync`/`dispatcher.sendToolResult` path in `dispatch-from-config.ts`, leaving a race condition where a session reset mid-await can still cause stale tool results to be delivered to the channel.

`isExternalProfileFresh` incorrectly returns `true` when `cred.expires` is missing or non-numeric, causing `shouldSync` to be skipped and leaving malformed or legacy profile entries permanently stuck without ever pulling fresh credentials from the external CLI.

The `autoObserve` feature's `shouldCaptureMemory` function lacks prompt-injection filtering, allowing malicious instruction-like text (e.g., "remember: ignore previous instructions") to be persisted and later re-injected as durable context, creating a persistent jailbreak vector.

When a shell profile is configured, `getShellConfig` incorrectly places the source prefix and the actual command in separate `-c` argv elements, causing POSIX shells like `sh`/`zsh` to ignore the real command and fail with a syntax error from the trailing `&&`.

If `manager.persist()` fails after `setBindingRecord()` has already stored the binding in memory, the stale in-memory binding is never cleaned up, causing the room/thread to keep routing messages to a session that never successfully started until the Matrix monitor restarts.

The streamed `/v1/responses` path omits `trustedProxies` when calling `runResponsesAgentCommand`, causing `resolveIngressSenderIsOwner` to always return `false` for streamed requests from trusted upstreams, while the non-stream branch correctly passes `trustedProxies` — creating a behavior mismatch that breaks owner-only capabilities for streamed responses.

A TOCTOU race condition in `AcpSessionManager.enforceConcurrentSessionLimit` allows concurrent session initializations to all pass the `maxConcurrentSessions` size check before any of them insert their entry into the runtime cache, causing the configured session limit to be exceeded during bursts of simultaneous session creation.

The `signalRecoverableOutboundNetworkError` method aborts `#activeFetchAbort`, which is shared across all Telegram API calls (not just `getUpdates`), meaning it can cancel in-flight `sendMessage` or `editMessageText` requests before the replacement poller is ready, causing the final reply to still be lost despite polling restarting.

In `resolveCronCallerOptions`, hardcoding `callerSessionKey` to `undefined` causes `callerOwnsJob` to reject every job for non-admin callers when `denyWithoutIdentity: true` is set, resulting in an empty `jobNameById` map and broken name-based run-log queries for those clients.

The rewrite of `web-search.ts` hard-codes provider selection to five built-in providers and removes the call to `resolveWebSearchDefinition`/`resolveWebSearchProviderId`, breaking runtime and plugin-owned provider overrides that were previously resolved through `resolveRuntimeWebSearchProviders` in `src/web-search/runtime.ts`.

The `wsConnect` function always hardcodes `ws://127.0.0.1:<port>` as the WebSocket connection target, ignoring the `host` value stored in `state.host`, which will cause connection failures in any deployment where the bridge is not running on loopback.

When an agent run reuses an idempotency key that was previously finalized, stale-packet guards can incorrectly drop the new run's packets if the first observed sequence number is greater than 1, causing the run to never stream or finalize until the tombstone expires.

The new album grouping path passes raw HTTP media URLs directly to Telegram's `sendMediaGroup` instead of fetching and uploading the bytes, causing silent failures for non-public or internally-reachable URLs (e.g., Tailscale or private CDN) that Telegram's servers cannot access, with no per-item fallback.

Calling `runPreflightCompactionIfNeeded()` before `runMemoryFlushIfNeeded()` causes the post-compaction token count to be marked fresh, which prevents the memory flush gate from triggering and silently drops durable note writes to `memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md` in the stale-usage scenario this patch intends to fix.

When an HTTP MCP session fails during `connect`, the error handling path skips cleanup of the partially initialized `Client` and transport, potentially leaking resources across retries — unlike the stdio path which correctly calls `disposeSession` on failure.
A duplicate `expectPreparedResult` function declaration in the Telegram test file collides with an existing one, causing a compile error that prevents the file from being typechecked or executed.

The `sendSmsViaNode` function always selects the first available Android node rather than the node that received the original inbound SMS, which in multi-node/multi-SIM deployments can cause replies to be sent from the wrong phone number or a node without service, breaking conversation continuity.

The new unknown-group warning path passes `resolvedThreadId` directly without filtering out the value `1`, which Telegram rejects, causing 400 errors in General-topic forums when `unknownGroupAction` is set to `"warn"` and silently skipping the warning before leaving when set to `"leave"`.

Conditionally skipping `clearPluginDiscoveryCache()` during snapshot loads allows the install-then-verify path in `resolveInstallableChannelPlugin()` to return stale cached data, causing a newly installed plugin to appear missing or outdated and breaking `channels add`/onboarding immediately after a successful install.

The `didSendViaMessagingTool` flag incorrectly suppresses the 429/529 mid-turn fallback reply for the originating conversation when a cross-target message was sent, because the coarse boolean doesn't distinguish between same-origin and cross-target sends, causing the originating conversation to silently receive no reply.

Setting `deliver: false` when `turnSourceChannel` or `turnSourceTo` is absent breaks session-fallback delivery for exec follow-ups, causing approved background executions on outbound channels like Telegram and WhatsApp to silently drop their completion messages instead of routing through the gateway's existing `session.lastChannel/lastTo` fallback.

The new `hasInlineImages` gate removes the previous `onlyHtmlAttachments` Graph fallback, causing messages whose media is referenced via HTML attachment tags or non-`` markup to silently return no media instead of attempting the Graph attachment download path.

Moving the `metadata.initialMessage` cleanup to after `await speak(...)` instead of before it creates a race condition where concurrent or duplicate stream connect events can trigger the initial greeting multiple times before the field is cleared.

The Vertex Claude 4.6 models (Opus and Sonnet) are hard-coded with a 200k context window instead of their documented 1M limit, causing premature compaction and prompt rejection for users on long-context workloads.

Changing `combinedMedia` to only use the last entry's media instead of flattening all entries causes silent data loss when multiple forwarded media messages are flushed together from the debounce buffer.

Removing the `next.totalTokens = undefined` line leaves a stale token count in place when a provider only reports partial usage data, causing the parent-fork guard to incorrectly treat compacted or model-switched sessions as exceeding the fork limit and skip history forking.

In `mode: "session"` spawns, the child relay captures the first `lifecycle start` matching `childSessionKey` regardless of `runId`, so a concurrent follow-up turn in the same child thread can hijack `observedChildRunId` and cause the parent to display the wrong output and completion while the original spawn is still pending.

The image model override applied when images are detected can be silently overwritten by a later `resolveChannelModelOverride()` call that doesn't account for the new image override flag, causing image uploads to fail by running on the wrong text model.

When a high-token session (tokensBefore >= 50000) enters the full summarization flow but fails due to model/API key resolution or a thrown error, it falls through to `return { cancel: true }` instead of writing a suppressing boundary, potentially triggering infinite compaction re-check loops as documented in issue #41981.

The `prewarmSessionUsageCache` function resolves model identity without passing the runtime subagent model fallback used by `buildGatewaySessionRow`, causing it to incorrectly skip cache warming for restored child sessions and reintroduce the transcript-read latency it was meant to eliminate.

When a compaction entry lacks the optional `firstKeptEntryId` field, the function silently returns an empty protected set instead of bailing out, allowing rolling pruning to incorrectly delete pre-compaction messages that may be semantically required to preserve transcript integrity.

During compaction, the session transcript path is unconditionally overwritten with a plain `<newSessionId>.jsonl` filename, which will silently break history, export, and status reads for any session that uses a non-canonical path such as timestamped fork files or topic-specific thread paths.

When `waitForMessageHandlerIdle()` times out during teardown, `forgetDiscordManagedBotIdentity` is called immediately while in-flight inbound jobs may still be running, causing those messages to nondeterministically lose `SenderManagedAccountId` metadata because the identity is cleared before all active jobs have finished resolving it.

The `extensions/deepinfra/index.ts` file imports `definePluginEntry` from `"openclaw/plugin-sdk/core"`, which is explicitly banned by the repo's guardrail test suite and will cause the Vitest run to fail before any DeepInfra functionality is tested.

Applying a shard matrix to the entire `secrets` job causes non-sharded steps like workflow auditing and `pnpm-audit-prod` to run 106 times instead of once, dramatically inflating CI cost and risking pipeline flakiness.

Removing the multimodal provider validation guard allows non-Gemini providers to silently fall back to embedding image/audio files as plain label strings instead of their actual contents, causing a quiet recall-quality regression for any installation using multimodal memory without a Gemini backend.

Because `readUsageFromSessionLog()` unconditionally sets `totalTokensFresh: true`, the newly added `logUsage.totalTokensFresh` condition always evaluates to true, causing transcript-derived token counts (which may omit cache or override tokens) to replace more accurate stored totals, leading `/status` and `session_status` to under-report context usage.

The `findNearestPluginSdkPackageRoot` fallback to `process.cwd()` no longer validates that the discovered package is an actual OpenClaw root, allowing any ancestor `package.json` that exports `./plugin-sdk/*` to poison `createJiti()` with alias entries from an unrelated workspace, causing `openclaw/plugin-sdk/*` imports to resolve to the wrong repo's files.

The new fallback that infers `messageId` from `toolContext.currentMessageTs` for `react`/`reactions` actions is effectively unreachable because the message-tool execution pipeline never propagates `currentMessageTs` into `toolContext`, causing Slack reaction calls that omit `messageId` to continue failing instead of using the inferred timestamp.

When passing images to vision-capable embedded models, each image is sent twice because the prompt still contains `[media attached: ...]` lines that aren't suppressed on the "skipped" path, and `detectAndLoadPromptImages` appends image references from the prompt to the already-populated `existingImages` without deduplication, doubling token usage and risking per-request image limits.

When `streamMessageId` is still `null` during the first in-flight preview send and `replyTargetDiverged` is true, the final reply is delivered immediately without awaiting or canceling the pending preview, causing a stray orphaned post to appear in the original thread if the in-flight send resolves afterward.

The stale buffer reaping logic incorrectly assumes all active chat runs have a corresponding `chatAbortControllers` entry, but runs created by `agent.request` and voice transcript ingress flows do not, meaning legitimate long-running runs that pause during tool calls can be wrongly treated as orphaned and have their buffered text deleted before the final event is received.

The empty-config guard checks `configuredProfiles.size` after store-derived IDs have already been added to it, allowing the guard to be bypassed in config-less setups and potentially deleting active store-only credentials that the guard was meant to protect.
The Supabase connectivity check hard-codes a query against a `users` table, causing valid credentials to appear invalid for any project that doesn't use that specific table name.

When using the Google embeddings provider, the configured `embedding.dimensions` override is ignored by `GoogleEmbeddings` but still used to size the LanceDB table, causing vector-size mismatch errors at runtime during `memory_store`/`memory_recall` operations.

The new `openclaw/plugin-sdk/signal` import duplicates symbols already imported from `./runtime-api.js`, causing duplicate local bindings (TS2300) that will break type-checking and building of the Signal channel package.

When `useAccessGroups` is set to `false`, the `authorizeDiscordVoiceIngress` function incorrectly omits the owner allowlist (`channels.discord.allowFrom`) from its authorization checks, allowing any guild member to use voice commands instead of restricting access to only the configured owners as documented.

An unconditional `shift()` on `pendingOrphanDeletes` during a skipped-turn media delivery can cause a later turn's successful send to delete an earlier turn's queued preview post, leaving users with no visible copy of that earlier turn's content.

The fallback resolution logic incorrectly clears `authProfileId` when providers differ but never restores it if a prior compaction override already cleared it, meaning that if a fallback resolves back to the original provider, profile-scoped credentials are lost and `getApiKeyForModel` may select the wrong or no API key.

The poll loop fails to recheck the `aborted` flag after `client.getChats()` resolves, allowing message handling and outbound replies to continue executing even after shutdown, causing unexpected side effects during restarts or gateway shutdowns.

Removing the session-store ancestry check (`getSubagentDepthFromSessionStore`) means legacy subagent sessions without a `subagent:` key prefix will be misclassified as external, causing internal orchestration completion messages to be incorrectly delivered to user-facing channels.

The GitHub Copilot token is only sanitized with `trim()` instead of the `normalizeSecretInput` function used by other auth paths, meaning embedded CR/LF characters can survive and get persisted to the profile, causing hard-to-diagnose authentication failures.
When eval cases fail, `runSkillTest` and `runSkillBench` do not return a non-zero exit code, causing CI pipelines to incorrectly report success even when test cases have failed.

After persisting followup queues to disk on shutdown, the in-memory queue is never cleared, causing duplicate message processing when in-process restarts replay both the live in-memory queue and the newly written disk snapshot.

Adding `.sh` and `.bash` extensions to the scannable set is insufficient because extensionless shell scripts with shebangs (e.g., `bin/install`, `postinstall`), common in npm packages, are still silently skipped by the extension-based `isScannable()` check, leaving a bypass for malicious code execution.

The `exec` job timeout configuration is ignored by the outer `resolveCronJobTimeoutMs()` wrapper, causing any exec job intended to run longer than the generic 10-minute ceiling to be incorrectly aborted regardless of its configured `timeoutSeconds`.

The fast-xml-parser security bump to 5.5.7 is only placed in `pnpm.overrides`, which is ignored by npm, leaving npm-based installs of the published CLI still vulnerable to resolving the older version through transitive dependencies.

When `blockStreamingDefault` is set to `"on"`, the new logic enables block streaming for Feishu plain-text replies, but because Feishu drops non-card block payloads and the agent runner suppresses the final payload after a successful block stream, ordinary non-card replies are silently lost.

The `unregisterSessionBindingAdapter` function's new adapterless fallback incorrectly uses `pop()` to remove the most recently registered adapter instead of deterministically removing the calling owner, which can leave a stale adapter active and cause session binding operations to route to the wrong manager.

The fallback logic incorrectly suppresses the normal closure pairing fallback for operators who have a stale shared/config token but a valid paired device token, because `resolveMutatingPairingFallbackAuth()` never accounts for the `AUTH_TOKEN_MISMATCH` device-token retry path in `GatewayClient`, leaving those users vulnerable to the restart failure the patch intended to fix.

Using colon-delimited `serverName:toolName` identifiers for bundled MCP tools causes them to fail the `TOOL_CALL_NAME_RE = /^[A-Za-z0-9_-]+$/` regex validator, which can silently drop assistant tool-call blocks during persistence and history replay, breaking tool-call/tool-result pairing and orphaning MCP tool interactions.

Although `sessionTarget` is now correctly propagated to the synthetic cron job, `runCronIsolatedAgentTurn` never actually uses it to resolve the target session, causing all non-`isolated` values like `"main"`, `"current"`, and `"session:<id>"` to behave identically and silently breaking operators who rely on those modes to resume a specific session.

Silently swallowing ENOENT errors for all package origins, rather than only for partially-installed bundled plugins, causes missing entry files in user-configured `extraPaths` packages to produce no candidates and no error, turning broken third-party packages or typos into undetectable silent no-ops.
