Moving `ensureRuntimeHandle` outside the `runTurn` try/catch block causes initialization failures to bypass failure bookkeeping, leaving observability counters and persisted session state inconsistent when early runtime setup throws an error.

When the gateway RPC send succeeds, the code exits early before `messageCommand()` can emit the success summary or `--json` payload, resulting in empty stdout and breaking the CLI contract for callers that parse or display send results.

The timeout detection logic only checks for `Error` objects as abort reasons, causing cron-driven timeouts that abort with a plain string (`"cron: job execution timed out"`) to set `aborted=true` but leave `timedOut=false`, silently skipping the timeout-specific handling branches.

The `isSafeToRetrySendError` function incorrectly returns `true` for grammY's "failed after N attempts" errors, which can represent ambiguous transport failures where Telegram may have already delivered the message, causing duplicate sends on non-idempotent retry paths.

When ACP thread bindings use a `"current"` placement for DM or current-surface spawns, the bootstrap delivery target is incorrectly reconstructed using `resolveConversationDeliveryTarget`, producing malformed recipients like `channel:user:<id>` that can cause the initial bootstrap message to be misrouted or fail.

Clearing the draft and resetting `hasStreamedMessage` during preview edit fallback causes the subsequent `deliverNormally()` call to lose its thread timestamp (because `replyPlan.markSent()` was already called), resulting in the fallback message being posted to the main channel instead of the correct thread.

The `memory-memoria` backend changes `memory_get` to only accept `memoria://` URIs, but the core system prompt still instructs the model to use file-path-style calls, creating a contract mismatch that can cause the agent to emit invalid `memory_get` requests at runtime.

The directive-tag stripping logic in `stripRawContentMeta` and `stripInlineDirectiveTagsForDisplay` is too aggressive — it removes `[[reply_to_current]]` and `[[reply_to:<id>]]` tags anywhere in the text rather than only at leading control-tag positions, causing legitimate user/assistant content that mentions these tags inline to be silently deleted and lost from session recall.

The normalization function collapses all role and channel mentions into generic tokens (`@role`, `#channel`), causing messages that differ only by target role or channel (e.g., `post this in <#ops>` vs. `post this in <#sales>`) to be incorrectly treated as duplicates and dropped within the 15-second deduplication window.

The lightweight dispatch path in `oag-diagnosis-lightweight.ts` calls `recordDiagnosis()` a second time after `requestDiagnosis()` has already appended a pending record, resulting in duplicate `diagnosisId` entries that cause phantom pending diagnoses in OAG history and prematurely evict real records from the 20-slot history limit.

The `onRecoverableSendChatActionNetworkFailure` callback is bound directly to the shared `TelegramPollingSession` without being scoped to its poll cycle, allowing a stale bot instance that outlives a restart to invoke `signalRecoverableOutboundNetworkError()` against a newer, healthy `activeRunner` and trigger a spurious restart.

When a streamed answer falls back to a normal send and sets `answerLaneNeedsBoundaryReset`, a subsequent final-only assistant turn without `onAssistantMessageStart` may incorrectly edit the stale preview message instead of sending a fresh message, corrupting the conversation transcript.

The `SENSITIVE_STOP_REASON_RE` regex only matches bare `stop_reason: sensitive` text and fails to handle JSON-formatted variants like `{"stop_reason":"sensitive"}`, causing safety-filter refusals to fall through to the generic error/failover path instead of returning the intended user-facing message.

The `unescapeXmlEntities` function double-decodes already-escaped XML entities (e.g., `&lt;` becomes `<` instead of `<`) because `&` is replaced before other entity patterns, silently corrupting tool payloads that contain intentionally escaped content.

Calling `resolveMatrixInboundRoute()` (which invokes `sessionBindingService.touch()`) before early-exit checks causes ignored messages like chatter, no-mentions, and unauthorized commands to reset `lastActivityAt`, preventing Matrix thread idle expiry and keeping sessions pinned to stale bindings indefinitely.

Adding `clientTools` enables `function_call` emissions, but because `previous_response_id` is never read and session keys are regenerated on each request, multi-turn continuations will silently land in a new agent session and lose conversation history.

Removing the `room:` prefix from conversation ID resolution breaks ACP reset routing for Matrix inbound contexts, causing `/new` and `/reset` commands in ACP-bound Matrix rooms to incorrectly reset the regular session instead of the bound ACP runtime.

The lockfile incorrectly drops `@lancedb/lancedb-linux-x64-gnu` from LanceDB's optional dependencies, which will cause `import("@lancedb/lancedb")` to fail at runtime on Linux x64 glibc systems due to the missing native binding.

The `resolveSessionEntry()` function fails to generate agent-scoped session key candidates (e.g., `agent:support:main`) when resolving `sessionKey:"current"`, causing `session_status` to incorrectly return "Unknown sessionKey" instead of the caller's own session for non-default agents.

The webhook handler calls `pullAndApplyWorkspaceSync` without passing the configured workspace directory, causing webhook-triggered syncs to write to the process default path instead of the gateway's configured agent workspace, which breaks sync consistency for deployments that define custom workspace paths.

The catch-all Copilot model resolver incorrectly advertises image input support for every unrecognized model ID, causing text-only or mistyped models to receive multimodal payloads and trigger provider-side failures instead of safely falling back to text-only behavior.

The poll loop fails to re-check the `aborted` flag after `await generateReply(...)` completes, allowing an in-flight reply to be sent via `client.sendMessage(...)` even after a restart or shutdown has been triggered.

XML entity sequences like `&`, `<`, and `>` are not unescaped before being parsed into tool call arguments, causing any MiniMax payloads containing characters such as `&`, `<`, or `>` to pass corrupted, escaped literals to the tool instead of the intended values.

The new try/catch blocks around cron operations return raw error messages directly to clients via `String(err)`, bypassing the gateway's centralized `formatForLog` sanitization and potentially exposing sensitive information like file paths or internal substrings.

For existing-session profiles, the new status logic incorrectly reports null/zero/empty values for `pid`, `cdpPort`, and `cdpUrl` because `profileState.running` is never populated for Chrome MCP-tracked sessions, breaking CLI and status-based automation for all preconfigured existing-session profiles.

The change unconditionally sets `deliver: false` when `turnSourceChannel`/`turnSourceTo` are absent, breaking session-key-only exec approval followups that previously relied on `gateway.agent` resolving the last external route from session state to deliver notifications correctly.

The image model override allowlist is built only from `agents.defaults.models` instead of the full allowed model set (including fallbacks), causing valid vision models approved via fallback chains to be incorrectly rejected and leaving image messages processed by a text-only model.

`loadConfig()` is called unconditionally before the remote/local branch check in `resolvePolicyConfigForRun()`, meaning a stale or invalid local config on the test runner will throw an error and abort live remote Android capability runs before they can fetch the policy config from the remote gateway.

The new `onPayload` hook inadvertently allows the Moonshot thinking wrapper to mutate native Ollama `/api/chat` request payloads with a non-native `thinking` object, breaking default Kimi cloud model requests that use the Ollama API.

The code incorrectly injects an implicit `default` account based solely on the presence of token-like fields in the base config, without verifying that the base account has all required channel-specific settings (e.g., `baseUrl` for Mattermost, `signingSecret` for Slack), which can cause account-less operations to resolve to an unconfigured and non-functional default account instead of a properly configured named account.

The OAuth refresh lock path sanitization replaces all non-alphanumeric characters with underscores, causing distinct profile IDs like `openai-codex:work/test` and `openai-codex_work:test` to collide on the same lock file, which can cause unrelated credential refreshes to incorrectly block each other and fail with a timeout.

The forget candidate list displays truncated 8-character IDs, but the delete flow requires full UUIDs, causing `memory_forget` to silently fail when users submit a displayed ID and breaking the two-step forget workflow.

The workflow fails to delete the `latest` git tag before recreating the release, causing subsequent pushes to `main` to publish a new release still anchored to the old commit rather than the new HEAD.

The queue entry normalization function omits `mirror.isGroup` and `mirror.groupId` fields, causing group delivery metadata to be silently lost on retry or crash recovery, which makes downstream hooks misidentify resent group messages as direct messages.

Inserting `applyMemorySearchDefaults` into the standard config pipeline causes read-only operations like `status` to unexpectedly trigger memory store initialization, creating SQLite files and starting file watchers on disk for users who never explicitly configured memory search.

In `mapContainerPathToWorkspaceRoot`, the "already a host path" fast path runs before container-path remapping, so bind mounts with `hostRoot: "/"` cause every absolute sandbox path to match and return unchanged, resulting in incorrect host path resolution where paths like `/host/etc/hosts` are used instead of the correctly remapped `/etc/hosts`.

The `resolveLoginProfiles` function renames the profile ID only after the GitHub Copilot provider has already written the hardcoded `github-copilot:github` profile to the auth store and config, resulting in a duplicate credential entry and the original unwanted profile persisting as the default.

Replacing the `@tloncorp/api` dependency with a `file:`-relative path will break installation of the published `@openclaw/tlon` plugin for any consumer outside the monorepo, since npm will attempt to resolve the tarball at a non-existent path relative to `node_modules/@openclaw/tlon`.

When `resolveServerChatModelValue` is called with a model ID that already contains a provider prefix (e.g., `openrouter/aurora-alpha`) alongside a matching `modelProvider`, it incorrectly double-prefixes the ID into `openrouter/openrouter/aurora-alpha`, causing the reported and cached model reference to not match the actual session model.

Eagerly persisting the old session entry with an updated timestamp before the new turn completes means that if the turn fails midway, the store will incorrectly treat the stale session as current, causing subsequent retries to resume the old session instead of creating the requested new one.

Queue objects created after `clearSessionQueues` is called during a session reset are not paused, allowing a new inbound message to create a fresh unpaused queue that drains against the old session before the store is updated, leaving the intended old-session race condition unresolved.

The fallback to `accountConfig.allowFrom` is only wired into the monitor's allow check but not the rest of the Matrix plugin, so users who set only the top-level key will be silently allowed through monitoring while status reporting, doctor, and onboarding still treat the allowlist as empty and may overwrite it with a separate `dm.allowFrom` list.

The newly added fields (`senderName`, `groupId`, `spawnedBy`, `sourceProvider`) are declared in `EmbeddedCompactionRuntimeContext` but are never actually passed through by the runtime builders in `attempt.ts` and `run.ts`, causing context engines to always see these fields as `undefined` and potentially leading to incorrect trust or policy logic.

The `mustWait` check only evaluates queue length and `lastDoneAt` without accounting for an already in-flight send, allowing a second `run()` call to bypass the throttle and execute concurrently while the first `fn()` is still running, defeating the intended serialization and rate-limit protection.

When multiple custom providers share the same base URL but have different credentials, a first-match lookup in `promptBaseUrlAndKey` may incorrectly reuse and persist an unrelated provider's API key into the new provider being configured.

Routing all strict fetches through the environment proxy when `HTTP_PROXY`/`HTTPS_PROXY` is set — without exempting callers that use `allowedHostnames`/`allowPrivateNetwork` for loopback or LAN hosts — will break local integrations and leak internal hostnames to the proxy in deployments where those hosts lack a matching `NO_PROXY` entry.

The memory-access guardrail script performs line-by-line regex matching, causing it to miss multi-line `writeFileWithinRoot` calls where the function name and `rootDir: memory` argument appear on separate lines, allowing direct memory writes outside the allowlist to silently pass CI checks.

When the oldest delivered message lacks a numeric timestamp, the cursor is set to null and hasMore is forced false, silently disabling pagination even when more messages exist beyond the current page.

Replacing the multi-match resolution helper with `Object.entries(...).find(...)` breaks deterministic session lookup, causing `session_status` to arbitrarily resolve to the first matching store entry rather than the preferred one when multiple keys share the same `sessionId`, risking stale or invalid session resolution.

The `thinking-events` capability check is bypassed for session subscribers because `sessions.subscribe` adds clients to `sessionEventSubscribers` without verifying `connect.caps`, allowing clients that connected without the `THINKING_EVENTS` capability to receive chain-of-thought reasoning frames.

When a bootstrap truncation warning is prepended to the prompt at runtime, the budget fields `currentPromptChars` and `reservedContextCharsEstimate` are calculated from the raw prompt without accounting for the extra warning text, causing context engines to under-reserve tokens and potentially assemble too much history, leading to overflow or compaction.

The `requireApiKey` flag is incorrectly derived from whether a provider is a plugin rather than from the actual handler being invoked, meaning a partial plugin override can bypass API key enforcement and cause runtime auth failures when the built-in handler is called with an empty key.

Adding a re-export of `slack.js` to `channel-runtime.ts` introduces a transitive runtime dependency on `@slack/web-api` into the generic channel SDK, causing import failures in any environment where Slack-specific packages are not installed.

Mapping all HTTP 404 responses to `model_not_found` is too broad, causing misconfigured base URLs or route typos to be misclassified as missing models, which can trigger incorrect failover retries and incorrectly cool down healthy credentials.

The pre-hook subprocess is not wired to the job's abort signal, so if the cron job times out, the shell command continues running in the background and can overlap with subsequent runs or cause unintended side effects after the job has already been marked as failed.

Silently catching exceptions from `os.networkInterfaces()` and returning empty arrays makes a host failure indistinguishable from "no tailnet IPs," causing the wide-area DNS-SD zone update to be silently skipped and leaving Tailscale-based remote discovery stale or broken.

Gemini's `thoughtsTokenCount` is never added to the normalized output or total token counts, causing thinking-enabled Gemini runs to silently underreport token usage and estimated costs in all downstream consumers.

When multiple documents with captions are batched together, only one caption is preserved as the `primaryEntry`, causing all other captions or instructions attached to subsequent documents to be silently dropped before reaching the agent.

The `checkAllRegistryProviders` function incorrectly filters out intentionally keyless plugin media providers during auto-selection because it routes all candidates through `checkProvider`, which requires `hasAvailableAuthForProvider`, preventing keyless providers from ever being chosen even though the execution path in `runProviderEntry` fully supports them.

`resolveNodeCommandAllowlist` is incorrectly used to validate `denyCommands` entries, causing legitimate deny rules for valid but non-allowlisted commands (e.g., `camera.snap`, `screen.record`) to be falsely flagged as unknown, undermining operator attempts to harden node access.

When a user explicitly enters a cleartext gateway URL on port 80, the display URL is rewritten to drop the port, causing a silent round-trip parsing bug that makes the app reconnect on the wrong port (18789) instead of the intended port 80.

The `trackingId` field is unconditionally included in video message payloads, but LINE rejects it for group and multi-person chat targets (prefixed with `C...`/`R...`), causing sends to those destinations to fail whenever a `trackingId` is set.

The new gateway RPC send path silently drops `--thread-id` and `--gif-playback` CLI flags when forwarding messages, causing messages to land in the wrong thread or videos to be sent without GIF playback instead of correctly passing these fields to the gateway's `send` method that already supports them.

Applying a blanket 30-second timeout to all async void hooks via `callHandlerWithTimeout()` will cause `agent_end` hook handlers—such as the `memory-lancedb` auto-capture path, which can require multiple sequential embedding calls within a legitimate 60s–5min budget—to silently drop memory capture on slower providers even when individual operations are within normal system limits.

In `trusted-proxy` mode, the browser control auth setup unconditionally overwrites `gateway.auth.token` with a new random plaintext token when no credential is resolved, which can silently replace a SecretRef-backed credential in `openclaw.json` and cause downstream flows to drift from the operator-managed secret.

The auth header lookup uses a strict byte-for-byte URL comparison to determine whether to reuse a saved provider's `authHeader: true` setting, so semantically equivalent URL variants (e.g., `https://host` vs. `https://host/v1`) will fail to match and cause onboarding to incorrectly fall back to `x-api-key` instead of `Bearer`, breaking verification against Bearer-only gateways.

When a Slack approval times out, forcing `decision: "deny"` locally clears the pending state prematurely, so if a real approval event arrives shortly after, it is silently ignored and Slack permanently displays "Denied" even though the gateway may have accepted an allow decision.

The replace-mode provider filter fails to map `volcengine-plan` and `byteplus-plan` variant IDs to their base providers, causing those catalog entries to be incorrectly excluded and potentially hiding the active default model from the dashboard's model selection dropdown.

The `shouldMarkTelegramNetworkHealthy` function incorrectly returns `false` for all 5xx responses, even JSON 5xx responses that confirm Telegram was successfully reached, which prevents `noteNetworkHealthy()` from being called and can cause the outage counter to spuriously trigger a polling restart on an otherwise healthy network.

On Windows, spawning `.cmd`/`.bat` wrapper scripts (e.g., `claude.cmd`, `npx.cmd`) directly via `child_process.spawn()` will fail on modern Node.js, because the code does not apply the existing `cmd.exe` routing logic that the repo already uses elsewhere for such executables.

Defaulting `supportsUsageInStreaming` to `true` based solely on provider ID (e.g., `vllm`/`sglang`) without verifying actual endpoint capability can silently enable `stream_options.include_usage` for incompatible backends, reintroducing the streaming failures the fallback logic was designed to prevent.

The inbound clone deduplication key is built solely from normalized message text, ignoring attachment identity, so two Discord messages with identical captions but different media files will incorrectly collapse into the same key and cause the later message to be silently dropped.

The `abb_robot_virtual` plugin sends `set_joints`/`movj` commands over a WebSocket connection, but the bridge implementation does not relay inbound frames between sessions, so these commands never reach the browser viewer and the virtual plugin cannot drive the 3D viewer at all.

Accepting negative chat IDs (e.g., `-100...`) as valid `allowFrom`/`groupAllowFrom` entries causes ordinary group messages to be incorrectly denied, because the group policy code treats any non-empty `effectiveGroupAllow` as a required sender allowlist and authorization still checks `msg.from.id` rather than the negative chat ID.

The catalog registers incorrect Vertex AI model IDs for Claude 4.5 models (missing the `@YYYYMMDD` date suffix format that Vertex requires), causing all requests that explicitly target those models or trigger the image fallback to Opus 4.5 to fail when sent to the Vertex API.

The `previewSendFailed` latch is never reset between assistant turns, so a single transient network error during streaming permanently disables in-place post editing for all subsequent turns in the same session.

Treating a missing `tenant_key` as a bot indicator incorrectly discards legitimate user mentions, causing `@Bot @User` forward requests to silently fail whenever Feishu omits `tenant_key` on a real user mention.

The `shouldUseAnthropicAuthorization` helper hardcodes `true` for `minimax`/`minimax-portal` provider IDs without consulting the merged provider config, so an explicit `authHeader: false` override set via `mergeProviders()` is silently ignored and the wrapper still strips `x-api-key` and forces `Authorization: Bearer` auth, breaking any proxy endpoint that was intentionally reconfigured to use API key authentication.

Adding "baidu" to the allowlist-compat IDs without a corresponding entry in `BUNDLED_WEB_SEARCH_PROVIDER_REGISTRY` means `resolvePluginWebSearchProviders()` never resolves Baidu, causing Baidu-configured setups to silently fall back to other providers and trigger missing-key failures at runtime.

Normalizing `sessionKey: "current"` to `requesterInternalKey` before gateway lookup prevents legitimate sessions whose key or ID is literally `"current"` from ever being resolved, silently redirecting those calls to the caller's own session instead.

After a `patchMattermostPost()` failure, `streamMessageId` is never cleared and `lastSentText` stays stale, causing the 200ms interval to repeatedly retry the failing patch API call for the entire duration of a long response, spamming logs and leaving the preview frozen on outdated text.

`closeHttpServer` calls `closeAllConnections()` before `server.close()`, creating a race window where new connections can still be accepted, potentially preventing graceful shutdown and causing the 5-second timeout to trigger while the port remains bound.

The `text_end` event is emitted before fallback tool JSON is stripped from `message.content`, causing raw JSON tool call syntax to be flushed to the user before the tool runs, even when the final event is correctly rewritten to `toolUse`.

When a newly targeted gateway unit is installed but unloaded, the old `unit_proxy` is never torn down before returning a synthesized `inactive/dead` state, allowing stale `PropertiesChanged` signals from the previous unit to corrupt tray state while Start/Stop actions already target the new unit.

The missing-entry suppression check uses `opened.reason === "path"`, which is too broad and silently swallows `ENOTDIR` and `ELOOP` errors in addition to the intended `ENOENT`, causing malformed packages with directory/symlink issues to fail silently without any diagnostic instead of only skipping genuinely missing files.

The `resolvedAssistantBubbleColor` function now returns a dark bubble color in both light and dark macOS appearances, causing very low contrast against dark assistant text in light mode and making chat messages unreadable.

Setting `senderIsOwner` to `false` over-broadly removes all cron tools (including non-mutating ones like `status`, `list`, `run`, `runs`, and `wake`) from isolated cron jobs, when the intent was only to prevent self-mutation via `cron.update`, causing legitimate read-only and trigger operations to fail unnecessarily.

The `autoFollowAll` task does not check the `aborted` flag during its async flow, causing it to continue issuing `followUser` mutations even after the channel has been shut down, resulting in unintended post-stop account actions.

The new `fetchLatestWhatsAppWebVersionViaProxy` fallback omits the required anti-bot headers (e.g., `User-Agent`, `sec-fetch-site`) that Baileys includes when fetching `sw.js`, causing WhatsApp to reject the request and silently fall back to the stale bundled version, meaning the login failures this change aims to fix will still occur.

A race condition between the asynchronous `initWebPushState` subscription detection and the single `reconcileWebPushState` call in `onHello` can cause existing browser push subscriptions to go unregistered on the gateway if the gateway connects before the local subscription state has finished resolving.

The documentation incorrectly claims that SecretRef objects are supported for media provider `apiKey` configuration, but the runtime code only resolves plain string values, meaning SecretRef configs will be treated as missing credentials and silently skipped.

The `findCodeRegions()` helper only recognizes triple-backtick fences, so a four-backtick code block is misidentified as an unclosed triple-backtick fence that masks everything through EOF, causing `splitMinimaxToolCalls()` to miss any `<minimax:tool_call>` wrapper that follows and silently drop the tool call instead of executing it.

The new branch unconditionally clears and resets any previously streamed message as if it were a draft, but because `hasStreamedMessage` is never reset after a draft is finalized into a real message via `editSlackMessage`, subsequent payloads in a multi-reply sequence will incorrectly delete already-posted final replies before sending the next one.

The cron field parser silently discards valid non-numeric Croner tokens (e.g., MON-FRI, JAN, L, #), causing jobs that use these expressions to incorrectly disappear from the timeline despite still being executed by the scheduler.

The shared test helper file imports only `vi` from Vitest but calls `expect(...)` throughout its exported functions, leaving `expect` undefined at runtime and breaking the entire image-generation test suite since Vitest globals are not enabled in the project config.

The `splitMinimaxToolCalls()` function's regex requires both opening and closing `<minimax:tool_call>` tags, causing malformed MiniMax payloads that only contain a closing wrapper tag to be silently dropped instead of parsed and executed as tool calls.

`ensureMemoryFlushTarget` unconditionally attempts to open and write the daily memory file on startup regardless of whether memory flush is enabled, which can cause `EACCES`/`EROFS` errors and abort valid runs on read-only workspaces even when the memory flush feature is disabled.

The `allowExec` guard is only checked in write mode, so dry-run and interactive preflight flows silently succeed even when a plan contains exec references, causing operators to complete the entire wizard before hitting a failure that requires rerunning with `--allow-exec`.

The new 30-second default hook timeout is lower than the 60-second budget allowed for remote embedding queries, meaning valid memory recall operations in the `memory-lancedb` plugin can be silently killed mid-flight, causing the agent to start without recalled memories even though no actual failure occurred.
The `viewOnce` flag is added to `ReplyPayload` but is never read or forwarded in the WhatsApp and Signal monitor delivery paths, so media intended to disappear will still be sent permanently.

The video understanding fallback model (`mimo-v2-omni`) is never used because the media runner always passes the active agent model (`mimo-v2-flash`) as `params.model`, which only supports text input, causing video description to silently use the wrong model on default Xiaomi installations.

The fix emits an assistant update for tool-call-only responses, but downstream relays in the gateway and agent stream handlers silently drop assistant events when the delta text is empty, so the tool-call-only signal still never reaches clients unless the emitted event is enriched with tool-call metadata or another non-empty marker.

The `refreshLock` function performs a single `handle.write(...)` call without retrying on partial writes, which can corrupt the on-disk JSON lock payload and cause other processes to incorrectly reclaim the lock after TTL expiry while the original critical section is still running.

When a new session's first turn fails before startup, the previous session's `skillsSnapshot` is incorrectly persisted, causing subsequent retries to reuse the stale snapshot and potentially miss SKILL.md installs/removals or environment changes that the `/new` session reset was intended to pick up.

Removing the `assertSchtasksAvailable` preflight check causes unhandled `execSchtasks` spawn errors (e.g., `ENOENT`) to escape `readScheduledTaskRuntime` before the Startup-folder fallback can be reached, turning graceful fallback/unknown responses into hard failures in environments where `schtasks` is unavailable.

`RunnerManager.launch()` calls `createRunner()` synchronously on the main Node thread, meaning the full model load and warmup (up to ~30 seconds) blocks the event loop and can stall unrelated services during cold ExecuTorch startup.

In `ensureCompositeWorkspace`, collision detection uses case-sensitive basename comparisons, so on case-insensitive filesystems (macOS/Windows) names like `Repo` and `repo` are treated as distinct during planning but resolve to the same on-disk symlink, causing one workspace to be silently dropped and allowing case-variant reserved names to bypass internal-name guards.

Returning `kind: "final"` for context overflow errors bypasses the post-run bookkeeping logic that only executes for `kind: "success"`, causing session metadata (usage, model, provider, system-prompt) to go stale and potentially skewing future token-based decisions and fallback behavior.

In sandbox mode, `wrapToolMultiRootGuard()` is incorrectly applied using stale host-side `resolvedRoots` paths, causing file operations inside the sandbox to fail with "outside allowed filesystem roots" errors because the resolved paths point to the sandbox copy of the workspace rather than the original host paths.

When parsing combined POSIX shell flags like `-cl`, the code incorrectly slices the characters after `c` within the same token (e.g., returning `"l"` as the command) instead of reading the command string from the next argv token, causing wrong command extraction and broken approval binding.

The `soulFile` field is never persisted to disk because the strict channel config schemas don't declare it, causing `writeConfigFile()`'s revalidation step to silently drop or reject the value and leaving users with a config-validation failure instead of a saved channel update.

Because `skills-status.ts` calls `loadWorkspaceSkillEntries()` without passing `agentId`, the new per-agent policy filtering is never applied during status lookups, causing agents that share a workspace to receive identical skill status reports regardless of their individual policy configurations.

The newly extracted `threadId` field is never forwarded through `extractMessagingToolSend()` into duplicate suppression logic, causing `shouldSuppressMessagingToolReplies()` to miss Telegram forum/topic sends and incorrectly emit duplicate assistant text when the tool has already posted to the same topic.

The `before` pagination cursor uses an inclusive boundary (`ts <= beforeTs`), causing the boundary message to be re-included on every page request, which results in duplicate history entries and can trap clients in an infinite non-advancing pagination loop.

The remediation command hardcodes `openclaw config set tools.fs.workspaceOnly true` instead of using `formatCliCommand()`, so users auditing a non-default profile will accidentally update the default config rather than the risky profile they intended to fix.

Unconditionally clearing `workspaceOverride` in `resolveConfiguredBindingRoute()` causes ACP rebinds for the same agent to silently drop a previously configured workspace override, falling back to the agent's default workspace on every turn after the first.

If a slow `sessions.create` RPC resolves after the user has already switched to a different session, the unconditional `switchChatSession` call will forcibly navigate them away from their current session, potentially losing any draft text or attachments they've started there.

The LINE setup code was changed to import from a relative `../../../src/line/accounts.js` path that only exists in a local repository checkout, causing module resolution failures in npm/distributed installs where the `src/` directory is not present in the published package layout.

The code ignores the `bindFunctions` callback returned by `api.render()`, which must be called after inserting the SVG into the DOM, causing any interactive Mermaid diagram features like tooltips and click bindings to silently break.

Using `origin.provider`/`origin.chatType` instead of `storeEntry.channel`/`storeEntry.chatType` for both fields fixes the `aggregates.byChannel` rollup but causes `sessions.usage` rows for bridged sessions to misreport the delivery channel (e.g., Telegram/Slack) as the originating channel (e.g., webchat), losing the delivery-side metadata that callers rely on for labeling and filtering.

Generating a new session ID after `resolveCronSession()` has already loaded the prior session entry does not achieve true isolation, because downstream functions like `resolveCronModelSelection()` and `resolveSessionAuthProfileOverride()` still read and propagate persisted model and auth state from the old entry.

By making `resolveHomeDir()` prefer `OPENCLAW_HOME`, service-manager paths for launchd and systemd are inadvertently redirected away from the real OS home directory, breaking daemon install, status, and management for any user who sets `OPENCLAW_HOME` to a non-home location.

Changing the install kind to `"pipx"` breaks skill installation because the parser only supports `brew|node|go|uv|download`, causing the install entry to be silently dropped and leaving users without a valid auto-install path for the skill.

The "🔧 Running" audit log event is emitted before allowlist and preflight validation checks, meaning blocked or failed commands will still appear in the log as if they successfully executed, making the audit trail misleading in failure cases.

The `bindTemplateData` function coerces all template substitutions to strings, causing numeric, boolean, and object fields bound via `${...}` expressions to lose their original types and render incorrect state (e.g., a numeric `ProgressBar.value` becomes `"0%"` instead of the correct percentage).

Marking 429 errors as retryable is unsafe because the retry infrastructure caps delays at 30 seconds with jitter, meaning retries will fire before Telegram's flood-control window expires, repeatedly hit rate limits, and exhaust all retry attempts.

When a circuit breaker trips, the wake is dropped from `pendingWakes` without scheduling a follow-up probe after `BREAKER_COOLDOWN_MS`, so recovery can be delayed far beyond the intended cooldown window in interval-driven deployments.

The trailing-ID fallback in `supportsXHighThinking` incorrectly grants `xhigh` thinking to any model whose last path segment matches an allowlisted ID regardless of provider, bypassing the provider/model gate and allowing unsupported `thinkingLevel: "xhigh"` configurations that will fail at execution time.

Setting `activateGlobalHookRunner: false` in the plugin CLI registration prevents the active plugin registry from being published, causing the pairing subcli to receive an empty channel list and incorrectly reject valid channels like `discord` as unsupported.

The fallback order for channel identity incorrectly prioritizes `Surface` over `Provider`, causing replies relayed through the web UI to be misrouted as internal webchat traffic instead of being attributed to the true source channel (e.g., Feishu).

If a socket disconnection is detected after one or two send failures, the retry logic exhausts its standard 3-attempt window and throws before it can switch to the extended reconnect-aware retry strategy, causing message loss in a stale-socket race condition.

The `retry_after` flag marks flood-control errors as retryable, but the retry runner caps delays at 30 seconds, causing premature retries when Telegram's required backoff meets or exceeds that cap and exhausting all retry attempts instead of honoring the server-directed wait.

When `combinedSignal` aborts during an `onEvent` await, the resulting `AbortError` is incorrectly routed through the generic turn-failure path, leaving the session in an `error` state instead of returning it to `idle` after a successful cancellation.

When an in-memory message hasn't been persisted yet, `readSessionMessages(...).length` returns a stale count, causing the broadcasted `messageSeq` to be incorrect (0 for new sessions or a duplicate on later turns), which breaks ordering and reconciliation for `session.message`/`sessions.changed` subscribers.

If a plugin getter throws during initialization, the truncated result gets permanently cached, causing built-in web-search providers to silently disappear for the rest of the process even after the module finishes initializing.

The zombie process check on macOS only matches exact strings "Z" and "Z+", but ps state fields can have additional modifier characters appended (e.g., "Zs", "ZE"), causing zombie processes to be incorrectly identified as alive and breaking stale lock cleanup.

Passing the rotated `newSessionId` only to `incrementCompactionCount` leaves the in-flight `followupRun` still bound to the pre-flush session, causing the same request to be split across two session files — the store points to the new transcript while the assistant reply is appended to the old one.

On macOS/BSD, `sed -i` requires an explicit backup suffix and will exit non-zero, causing the script to abort prematurely under `set -e` before any dist files are patched, breaking the skill on macOS despite it being documented as a general-purpose workflow.

The fix for resolving built-in channel names from session keys silently falls back to `(unknown)` in production because the `sandbox` command is excluded from plugin loading and `normalizeAnyChannelId` depends on an empty plugin registry at runtime, even though tests pass due to a pre-seeded fake registry in `test/setup.ts`.

When `selectedToActive` baking is enabled, the script incorrectly deselects all objects and reselects only the target mesh, leaving no source objects selected and causing the high-to-low poly bake path to fail or produce incorrect results.

The refactored profile creation code drops normalization support for the `"existing-session"` driver type, causing any client that passes `driver="existing-session"` to silently fall through and create a wrong managed CDP profile instead of the intended attach-only Chrome MCP profile.

Because `tenant_key` is an optional field in the Feishu event contract, `isLikelyBotMention()` incorrectly classifies real user mentions with a missing `tenant_key` as bot mentions, causing legitimate forwarding flows to be silently dropped.

Hook script paths are resolved relative to the cron store's parent directory instead of `ocHomeDir`, causing `beforeRun`/`afterRun` hooks to fail with module-not-found errors when `cron.store` is configured outside the default state directory.

The newly emitted `usage` event for queued follow-up runs loses its session mapping because `emitAgentEvent` drops the `sessionKey` for non-internal channels and the run context is already cleared by the time the event fires, meaning session-scoped recorders will never receive usage data for queued follow-up completions.

The `--theme` CLI flag does not reliably apply the requested theme because importing the theme module before calling `setTheme()` freezes color functions (e.g., `theme.fg`, select-list colors, syntax highlighting) to their pre-override values, causing most UI elements to render with the wrong theme at runtime.

Setting `defer: false` removes the early Discord acknowledgment, meaning any component callback that takes longer than ~3 seconds to respond (e.g., due to network or disk I/O in a plugin handler) will exceed Discord's interaction timeout and show users "This interaction failed."

The new preflight compaction call in `followup-runner.ts` can compact the transcript before a queued turn without first invoking `runMemoryFlushIfNeeded()`, causing durable memory notes to be silently skipped for busy sessions that go through the followup queue's stale-usage path.

The `resolveSkillPath` function only looks up skills in `<workspace>/skills/<name>`, failing to check all other supported skill discovery locations (managed skills, `~/.agents/skills`, `<workspace>/.agents/skills`, and configured extra/plugin dirs), causing `skills test|bench|analyze-trigger` subcommands to incorrectly report skills from those locations as missing.

The `rotateDeviceToken` function's scope-escalation error return was changed from the established `{ ok: false, reason: "..." }` contract to a new `{ error, message, details }` shape, breaking existing callers and tests that still branch on the `ok` field.

A condition change that requires a colon (`:`) in room ID keys causes bare `!roomId` entries in matrix room/group config to be incorrectly routed through `resolveMatrixTargets` instead of being retained verbatim, silently dropping room-specific allow/deny and prompt settings for those rooms.

When sending media with a caption, a local media path allowlist violation is only caught after the caption text has already been sent, causing a partial send where retrying will duplicate the caption and leaving callers unable to treat the failure as a clean no-op.

When a transparent wrapper command (e.g., `nice`, `nohup`) is unwrapped recursively, the `allow-always` decision gets stored against the inner executable rather than the outer wrapper, causing approval reuse to break for subsequent invocations that still present the wrapper as the first token.

When a session migrates to the new `totalTokensEstimate` field, a zero-usage or missing-usage run clears `totalTokens` without seeding `totalTokensEstimate` from the legacy value, causing the token count to be lost entirely for pre-existing sessions instead of being preserved.

When the user selects "Skip for now" in the search setup, the config is still silently mutated by appending `web_search`/`web_fetch` to the allowlist and potentially toggling `tools.web.fetch.parallel.enabled`, because the post-search mutation block executes based on the presence of an existing provider rather than whether the user actually made a new selection.

Blocking `GIT_SSH_COMMAND` in `blockedKeys` incorrectly strips the operator's legitimate SSH configuration from all child processes, breaking agent git workflows for SSH remotes, when the real security concern is only agent-supplied overrides rather than the inherited host environment.

The cache boundary marker is inserted after `extraSystemPrompt`, which contains per-turn group chat content that changes between messages, so the supposedly stable cached prefix will still be invalidated on every turn in group chats, negating the intended caching optimization.

The unconditional TTY check in `modelsAuthLoginCommand` blocks the new headless device-code login flow from ever running in non-TTY environments like CI or SSH sessions, even though that flow doesn't require an interactive terminal.

Forwarding `workspaceOverride` into cross-chat `message()` sends causes Discord interactive components to persist and restore the source conversation's workspace instead of the destination's, reintroducing the workspace bleed the feature was designed to prevent.

In the timeout-triggered compaction path, `after_compaction` hooks are fired before `runPostCompactionSideEffects()` completes, meaning hooks may observe stale pre-compaction state or report success even if subsequent side effects fail, unlike the normal compaction flow where side effects are guaranteed to complete first.

The `anthropic-vertex` reference stripping applied to PDF model config resolution is not also applied in `image-tool.ts`, meaning an explicit `agents.defaults.imageModel: anthropic-vertex/...` config will still reach `runImagePrompt()` and fail at runtime with "No media-understanding provider registered for anthropic-vertex".

When subtracting one month from dates on the 29th–31st, `setUTCMonth(date.getUTCMonth() - 1)` can overflow into the current month (e.g., March 31st becomes March 3rd), causing `startPublishedDate` to be only days in the past instead of a full month, making month-filtered Exa searches return far more recent results than intended.

The skill only collects a bare branch name for PR creation, but `gh pr create` requires the `<user>:<branch>` format when the head branch lives on a fork, making it impossible to reliably create PRs from forked repositories.

The `truncateToBytes()` function appends a truncation marker (e.g., `"…[truncated]"`) after already trimming to `maxBytes`, causing the final string to exceed the intended byte limit and potentially causing oversized attributes to still be rejected by collectors.

When a source-checkout rebuild is triggered, the child `spawn()` call drops critical Node.js runtime flags (e.g., `--max-old-space-size`, `--conditions`) from `process.execArgv` because they are only restored later in `runOpenClaw()`, meaning the rebuild process may fail if it depends on those flags to run correctly.

Returning `false` from `validateLink` causes markdown-it to skip token emission for dangerous URLs (e.g., `javascript:`, `file:`), bypassing the custom image/link flattener and rendering rejected URLs as raw markdown source instead of sanitized label text.

When mirroring webchat messages that use `[[reply_to_current]]`, the helper only passes raw `text` to `deliverOutboundPayloads`, which cannot resolve `replyToCurrent` on its own, causing replies intended for the current external thread to be incorrectly sent as new top-level messages.

The `mentionedJids` values are passed directly into `contextInfo` without being normalized through `toWhatsappJid()`, meaning non-standard identifier formats like `+15551234567` or `whatsapp:+15551234567` will never be converted to valid WhatsApp JIDs (e.g., `15551234567@s.whatsapp.net`), causing mentions to silently fail.

A race condition in the inline query handler allows a stale fallback auto-edit from the preview LLM to overwrite already-completed research output when the user triggers "Research more" before the preview timeout resolves.

The `num_ctx` option is extracted from `params.model` and passed to `installToolResultContextGuard()` unconditionally, meaning non-Ollama or OpenAI-compat models with `injectNumCtxForOpenAICompat: false` will have their tool results compacted against an incorrect synthetic context budget, potentially causing premature compaction or overflow on otherwise valid turns.

Relative preload/import flags in `process.execArgv` (e.g., `--import ./loader.mjs`) are broken by the respawn logic because it changes the child process's `cwd` to `packageRoot`, causing Node.js to resolve those relative paths against the wrong directory instead of the caller's original working directory.

Adding the `onPayload` hook to the native Ollama request path unintentionally activates Moonshot compatibility wrappers that inject OpenAI-style fields (e.g., `thinking`, `tool_choice`) into native `/api/chat` payloads, potentially malforming requests for Kimi Cloud models that use `provider="ollama"`.

The newly added `headingAware` chunking option is read by `mergeConfig()` but is missing from the Zod runtime schema, so any config that attempts to enable it will be rejected at load time and the feature can never be activated through normal configuration.

The `resetGigachatBaseUrl` check is hardcoded to only inspect the `gigachat:default` profile, so if an active Basic-auth profile with a custom host is configured via `auth.order` or another profile ID, switching to OAuth during onboarding will incorrectly preserve the stale Basic endpoint, causing all subsequent OAuth requests to be routed to the wrong host.

When `getStatusCode(err)` returns nothing, `shouldTriggerFallback()` falls back based solely on `reason !== null` regardless of mode, causing custom status allowlists to be silently bypassed for status-less `FailoverError`s (e.g., `model_not_found`), which can unintentionally exhaust alternate models on errors the user never opted into.

The new `gateway.staticFiles` config option was added to the TypeScript type but never registered in the Zod validation schema, causing any deployment that attempts to use it to be rejected at config validation time.

The `createAnthropicAuthHeaderWrapper` relies on `authHeader` being set in the explicit provider config, but for built-in Bearer-only providers like `minimax`, this flag is only injected by `resolveImplicitProviders()` and is absent when users have no explicit `models.providers` entry, causing those endpoints to incorrectly use Anthropic-style auth instead of Bearer auth at runtime.

The `routeLogsToStderr()` call is applied too late in the execution path — after `enableConsoleCapture()` has already been skipped for routed commands — meaning diagnostic logs can still leak to stdout and corrupt JSON output for commands like `memory status --json`.

When `compaction` is set to `false`, the preemptive context overflow signal is never raised, causing over-budget prompts to be submitted to the provider anyway and triggering a costly failed prefill before session compaction kicks in — defeating the purpose of the flag.

Loading all media upfront in `loadAndClassifyMedia` before any send call means a single failed media URL will abort the entire 2-10 item media group, eliminating the previous partial-delivery behavior where successfully loaded items were still sent.

The `startsWith(trimmedProvider + "/")` check incorrectly treats provider-prefixed model IDs (e.g., `openrouter/hunter-alpha`) as already-qualified refs, causing the resolved server value to diverge from the catalog option key (e.g., `openrouter/openrouter/hunter-alpha`) and breaking model selection in the picker.

When `agentId` is passed to `readRecentMessages()`, it is silently ignored because `resolveSessionFilePathOptions` and `resolveSessionFilePath` prioritize `storePath` over `agentId` when resolving the transcript directory, causing the function to return the wrong agent's history or an empty array instead of the target agent's conversation history.

When promoting the `channel` field to `target` during normalization, the original `channel` value is never cleared, leaving an invalid provider identifier that causes `resolveMessageChannelSelection` to reject it and the send action to continue failing instead of recovering.

Because `streamedTurnCount` is incremented before `patchMattermostPost()` completes, a failure in boundary finalization causes `deliver()` to incorrectly skip the older turn instead of re-delivering it, risking silent message loss.

The new `viewOnce` flag is accepted at the schema level for all channels but is silently ignored by unsupported channels like Telegram, causing callers to falsely believe disappearing media was applied when it was not.

The health check watchdog that restarts stale connections only calls `stopRunner()` without a `forceCyclePromise` fallback, so a hung update handler can block `runner.task()` indefinitely and prevent the restart from ever completing.

The normalized "ok" status is only applied to persisted job state but not propagated to emitted "finished" events, causing operators to still receive erroneous "error" notifications and run-log entries in the delivered-but-follow-up-error case the patch intends to fix.

Setting `compat.supportsStore = false` unconditionally for all Azure custom models blocks the Responses compaction path permanently, causing user-configured `responsesServerCompaction: true` to silently have no effect and denying large-context Azure sessions access to a documented workaround.

Because `normalizeLanguageCode` only accepts 2-letter ISO-639-1 codes, xAI-valid language values like `auto` or `pt-BR` throw an error before they can populate `overrides.xai.language`, causing most xAI language overrides to be silently dropped before synthesis begins.

The service layer was updated to accept `payload.kind="exec"` for cron jobs, but the gateway RPC schemas and validation logic were not updated to match, making the new payload type silently inaccessible to external callers through the public API.

The injected `OPENCLAW_SESSION_KEY` is added to the merged `env` but is not forwarded through the node-host code path, causing skill scripts to silently fail to discover the session key during `host=node` executions.

Adding a `postinstall` script referencing `postinstall.mjs` to `package.json` will cause Docker builds following the documented steps to fail with `Cannot find module '/app/postinstall.mjs'` because the existing `COPY` instructions in the Docker documentation don't include `postinstall.mjs` before `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` is run.

The empty-config safety guard checks only `configOnlyProfileIds.size` and misses providers explicitly disabled via empty `auth.order` arrays, causing `models auth clean` to abort cleanup and leave stale credentials for those disabled providers instead of removing them.

When `seedream_generate` returns an error status, the script only prints a message and exits with code 0 instead of a non-zero exit code, causing automation and wrappers to incorrectly treat failed image generations as successes.

After a timeout-triggered compaction, the code unconditionally retries the prompt even if partial replies were already streamed to the user, which can produce duplicate or conflicting messages on surfaces like Discord and Slack.

The `/set_topic_name` command fails to include `MessageThreadId` when composing session labels, causing two DM topics in the same chat with the same topic name to generate identical labels, which results in a `label already in use` error or ambiguous label resolution on the second `sessions.patch` call.

If a run completes without a final payload before the first 200ms timer tick, the no-final cleanup guard is bypassed because `streamMessageId` and `patchSending` are still falsy, causing the `patchInterval` to fire post-settlement, post a stray preview message to Mattermost, and never be cleared.

The `registry.has(normalizedKey)` guard in `buildMediaUnderstandingRegistry` incorrectly prevents `describeImage` from being attached to pre-seeded core providers (e.g., `groq`, `deepgram`), so even when a config declares image-capable models for those providers, the image tool remains unavailable because `describeImage` is never registered.

`mediaLocalRoots` is wired into `createFeishuReplyDispatcher` but never passed at either call site in `bot.ts`, leaving it `undefined` at runtime and causing agent-scoped local image paths to fail the allowlist check and degrade to raw path text instead of media.

The OpenRouter media understanding provider hardcodes the global `fetch` instead of using the proxy-aware fetch utility, causing image analysis requests to bypass any configured `HTTPS_PROXY`/`HTTP_PROXY` and fail in proxy-dependent environments.

Overriding markdown-it's `validateLink()` to allow all non-`data:` URLs bypasses its built-in scheme blocklist (e.g., `javascript:`, `file:`), causing unsafe or unresolvable links to become inert but visually misleading anchors in the UI after DOMPurify strips the href.

The helper builds session keys using simple lowercasing instead of the canonical `normalizeAgentId` logic, causing `peekSystemEvents` to look up a different key than the one used during enqueueing, which makes `status` silently report an empty queue even when pending events exist for agent IDs containing spaces or punctuation.

Prepending bootstrap warning text to `params.prompt` for all runs, including heartbeat-triggered ones, breaks the exact-match shape the system prompt heartbeat rule requires, causing the model to return regular responses instead of `HEARTBEAT_OK` and producing avoidable heartbeat noise when bootstrap truncation occurs.

The `onPayload` hook is called in `createOllamaStreamFn` but its return value is ignored, meaning any payload replacements returned by the hook are silently discarded and the original unmodified `body` is always sent to Ollama.

The build-unsigned.yml workflow skips required toolchain setup steps (such as swiftformat, swiftlint, and xcodegen) that are present in ci.yml, causing the job to fail on a clean macos-latest runner with missing-command errors before an IPA can be produced.

The new skill file committed under `skills-tchif/` will never be discovered or loaded because the skill loader only scans specific directories (e.g., `skills/`, `.agents/skills/`, configured extra dirs), making the capability completely inaccessible in a default setup.

On a cache miss, the code falls back to the synchronous `resolveModelWithRegistry()` path instead of the async `resolveModelAsync()` path, meaning OpenRouter models that require `prepareDynamicModel()`/`loadOpenRouterModelCapabilities()` to populate image-support capabilities will incorrectly fail with "Model does not support images" on first use.

In `resolveDefaultConfigCandidates`, the nested-home guard calls `resolveStateDir(env)` without forwarding the caller-supplied `homedir`, causing tilde expansion to use the real OS home instead of the injected one, which makes the equality check fail and silently skips valid flat config candidates for non-default home directories.

The `Action.ToggleVisibility` handler queries elements by `data-ac-id` attribute, but the renderer never emits that attribute on card elements, so toggle actions can never find their targets and will always silently no-op.

The removal of the `shouldRetryTurnWithFreshHandle` logic from the catch path means transient early `acpx` exits no longer trigger an automatic retry with a fresh runtime handle, causing users to receive hard `ACP_TURN_FAILED` errors instead of recovering transparently on a second attempt.

Replacing exact model ID matching with `modelIdMatchesDotDash` in `applyConfiguredProviderOverrides` can apply the wrong headers, costs, or token limits when both dotted and dashed variants of a model ID exist in `providerConfig.models`, because the fuzzy match may select whichever equivalent entry appears first instead of the intended exact match.

The new `hasTelegramRetryAfterParameter` retry branch incorrectly allows retrying too early because the retry engine caps delays at 30 seconds and applies negative jitter, meaning Telegram-mandated `retry_after` windows longer than 30 seconds (or those reduced by jitter) are not fully respected, risking repeated 429 errors until all retry attempts are exhausted.

The inline `/model` switch guard compares only cached token totals against the budget threshold without accounting for the current prompt's tokens, allowing a session near the limit to accept a mixed model-switch message and overflow on that same turn.

When a user selects the OAuth flow but provides Basic credentials (e.g., "user:pass"), the interactive GigaChat path stores them without validation under `authMode: "oauth"`, causing all GigaChat requests to fail instead of falling back to Basic auth as the non-interactive path correctly handles.

When `model.baseUrl` is undefined, `isDirectOpenAIBaseUrl` incorrectly returns false, causing `function.strict` to be stripped from native OpenAI requests that rely on the default endpoint, which can break structured tool calling for configurations that should fully support strict mode.

The `voicewake.routing.set` handler lacks sufficient input validation, allowing malformed payloads to silently overwrite the persisted routing configuration with coerced defaults instead of returning an `INVALID_REQUEST` error.

Setting `deliver: false` for webchat-only exec approval followups silently discards the command result, because the delivery and transcript pipeline never routes a `session.message` event back to subscribed webchat clients, leaving users with no confirmation that their approved command ran.

Using `isExecEventReason` to gate the `forceLastTargetWhenNone` override is too broad, as it is true for non-completion exec events like `exec.started` and `exec.denied`, causing heartbeats with an explicit `target: "none"` to incorrectly fall back to the last delivery channel and produce unintended user-facing notifications.

Changing `@noble/ed25519` from an exact version (`3.0.0`) to a range specifier (`^3.0.0`) without regenerating `pnpm-lock.yaml` will cause `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` to fail with `ERR_PNPM_OUTDATED_LOCKFILE`, breaking CI and any clean environment setup.

Resolving the default timezone on the CLI client side instead of the remote gateway causes cron jobs to be silently scheduled using the operator's local timezone rather than the gateway's timezone, leading to incorrect job timing in remote deployments.

The newly added `fallbackOnErrors` parameter in `runWithModelFallback` is never actually read or used in the implementation, making it a no-op that silently fails to change which HTTP error codes trigger a model fallback retry.

The supersession checks in `createFollowupRunner` halt before delivery but `sendFollowupPayloads()` never re-checks supersession or passes an abort signal during its send loop, allowing stale output from a replaced session to leak through slow channel sends via `routeReply()` or `opts.onBlockReply()`.

The new `DiscordIdSchema` coercion accepts any JavaScript number (including floats, negatives, and exponent-form values) rather than only valid integer snowflakes, so invalid IDs like `-1` or `1.5` silently pass validation and are stored as strings that will never match in downstream Discord allowlist checks instead of surfacing a config error.

When `resolveTelegramReplyId()` fails to parse an explicitly supplied reply target ID, the new `?? params.defaultReplyToId` fallback silently redirects the reply to the current inbound message instead of leaving it unthreaded, causing the bot to reply to the wrong message.

`splitLongLineByBytes` splits strings using UTF-16 code unit indices without checking for surrogate pair boundaries, which can corrupt multi-byte Unicode characters (such as emoji) by producing chunks with unpaired surrogates.

The tolerant parsing path skips the `normalizeLegacyWebSearchConfig` step that strict validation performs, causing legacy web-search config keys to be misidentified as unrecognized, pruned from the config, and silently dropped during gateway startup.

The `normalizeAliasKey` loop selects the first present alias regardless of value quality, so if an earlier alias exists but is empty or undefined, it gets copied to the canonical key and iteration stops, causing downstream tool calls to receive invalid parameters even when a later alias in the list holds a valid value.

If a model emits a stray `<tool_call>` opener in explanatory text before a real tool call, the parser incorrectly pairs it with the closing tag of the genuine call, causing JSON parsing to fail and the cursor to advance past the real tool call, resulting in no tool calls being recovered.

When re-onboarding an existing Azure custom model, only `contextWindow` is updated while new fields like `reasoning`, image input support, and `compat.supportsStore` are never applied, leaving stale model metadata that breaks dependent functionality.

When `preflightDiscordMessage()` returns `null` due to transient failures (e.g., REST hydration errors), the message ID is not cleared from `recentInboundMessages`, causing legitimate redeliveries of the same message to be incorrectly dropped for up to 5 minutes.

Moving the reachability probes before `toBrowserErrorResponse` handling means that if `isHttpReachable()` or `isReachable()` throws a `BrowserProfileUnavailableError`, the error bypasses the proper JSON 409 error mapping and falls through to the generic framework error handler, causing `browser status` to lose its actionable attach-failed response.

The `shouldInjectBedrockBearerWrapper` guard incorrectly gates on profile presence rather than the actual resolved auth path, causing Bedrock requests to be sent without the `Authorization: Bearer` header when a stale or invalid profile exists but auth falls back to `AWS_BEARER_TOKEN_BEDROCK`, resulting in authentication failures.

The session reset capability is captured once at registry construction time and cached without `coreGatewayHandlers` in the cache key, so a registry built without `sessions.reset` support can be incorrectly reused in a later gateway context where the reset method is available, causing `api.resetSession` to permanently fail.

When `findExtraGatewayServices()` detects a system-scoped gateway unit (in `/etc/systemd/system`, etc.), the hint builder incorrectly generates a `--user` scoped `systemctl` command instead of a system-scoped one, causing the doctor output to display a cleanup recipe that appears correct but fails to actually remove the conflicting service.

After setting `activate: false`, the snapshot registry returned by `loadOpenClawPlugins()` is never passed to `listChannelPlugins()`, so `channels.*` schema and UI metadata are still built from the stale active registry, leaving channel configuration incorrect until the gateway restarts.

The HTTP 429 rate-limit retry wrapper is only applied to the normal prompt path, leaving compaction sessions in `compact.ts` unprotected so that transient rate-limit errors during compaction will still fail immediately instead of backing off.

The test runtime mocks were renamed from `describeMessageTool` to `listActions`/`getCapabilities` before the contract suite's discovery logic was updated to match, causing Telegram and Discord channel tests to return null for actions and capabilities instead of the expected contract surface.

Setting `input: ["text", "image"]` on Copilot models is insufficient because `loadModelCatalog` doesn't include GitHub Copilot rows, causing `src/media-understanding/runner.ts` to incorrectly route vision-capable models like `gpt-5.4-mini` through the OCR/vision preprocessing fallback instead of passing images natively, adding unnecessary latency and cost.
Routing memory flushes onto the shared `AGENT_LANE_SUBAGENT` lane causes auto-replies to block indefinitely behind unrelated spawned subagent work on that lane, even when the main agent lane is idle.

When `imageModelFallbacks` is undefined (e.g., the image model is configured without fallbacks), the code silently falls back to the text-only model fallback chain, which cannot handle image attachments and will reproduce the original failure instead of correctly failing fast or using only image-capable models.

After a patch interval is stopped due to a send/edit failure, `onPartialReply` can still invoke `schedulePatch()` on subsequent deltas, which recreates the interval timer whenever `patchInterval` is null, causing repeated failing API calls and log spam for the duration of the stream instead of halting retries after the first failure.

Once `isLegacy` flips true after an `assemble.runtimeContext` rejection, the compatibility wrapper never retries `invokeWithLegacySessionKeyCompat` for subsequent lifecycle hooks (`afterTurn`/`compact`), causing older plugins that validate different hook params differently to either lose `runtimeContext` entirely or surface unhandled `sessionKey` validation errors.

The new `optionKeys.has(trimmedModel)` branch incorrectly strips the wrapper provider (e.g., "openrouter") from hierarchical model IDs like "anthropic/claude-haiku-4.5" when the catalog contains a matching direct provider key, causing the wrong provider to be submitted in environments where both a wrapper and underlying provider are configured.

The unescape callback's fallback branch drops the leading backslash from unrecognized escape sequences, corrupting non-ASCII characters (e.g., `\u2019` becomes `u2019`) when NIM emits unicode escapes inside serialized text blocks.

The script overwrites Alibaba's documented 1M context window values for `qwen3.5-plus`, `qwen3-coder-plus`, and `MiniMax-M2.5` with smaller limits (262144/204800), which actually reduces the available prompt budget and causes long-context chats to compact or truncate earlier than intended.

The unconditional `loadFreshConfig()` call during Telegram message context construction can throw if the config file is temporarily unreadable (e.g., mid-edit), causing all inbound messages to fail instead of gracefully falling back to the already-loaded startup configuration.

Recursive `executeWorkflow()` calls share the same `tokenTracking` object, so adding `trueChainResult.tokenTracking` back into `this.tokenTracking` at lines 333–339 double-counts token usage for every child step, causing any multi-step workflow to report inflated token and cost totals.

The new call to `hasEnvHttpProxyConfigured` always uses the HTTPS lookup path regardless of the actual request protocol, causing `http://` fetches to incorrectly enter env-proxy mode when only `HTTPS_PROXY` is set, resulting in direct-connect failures in proxy-only environments.

When `applyGigachatConfig()` is called without a `baseUrl` during re-authentication, it unconditionally overwrites any existing custom GigaChat endpoint with the default `GIGACHAT_BASE_URL`, silently redirecting future requests to the wrong server.

After a hot-reload of browser config, `createBrowserRouteContext.forProfile()` still reads `defaultProfile` from the pre-refresh snapshot, so the first request that omits a profile can use a stale or removed default, causing a "Profile not found" error even though the new default is already loaded in state.

The streamed preview post is deleted before the fallback delivery is confirmed successful, meaning a failure in `deliverMattermostReplyPayload` leaves the user with no visible response at all instead of preserving the partial reply.

Although `loadFreshConfig` is wired in, `resolveTelegramGroupConfig` still closes over the stale startup config snapshot, meaning runtime edits to direct/topic overrides (e.g., `dmPolicy`, `allowFrom`, `requireTopic`, `agentId`) are never picked up without a full bot restart.

The new override that silently corrects a non-loopback bind host when Tailscale serve/funnel is enabled is unreachable in practice because upstream startup config validation still fatally rejects that exact configuration before `resolveGatewayRuntimeConfig` is ever called.

In the retry loop, post-compaction context is appended to `effectiveExtraSystemPrompt` on every compaction attempt rather than replaced, causing up to three duplicate copies of the same ~3000-character AGENTS.md refresh to accumulate in the system prompt and undermining the token budget that compaction was meant to recover.

The `wrapOutbound` function calls detached method references without preserving their original `this` binding, which will cause runtime failures for any listener implementations that rely on `this` context, and should instead use `.apply()` with the receiver to safely wrap outbound calls.

When a pre-tool-call assistant turn contains both text and media attachments, an early return in the streaming path silently drops those attachments because the only code that uploads them is bypassed once the turn is marked as already streamed.

The silent failure detection incorrectly treats intentional `NO_REPLY` outcomes as errors, causing a fallback error message to be sent to users even when the agent is legitimately configured to stay silent.

Adding a second `ChannelPlugin` import from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/signal` in `channel.setup.ts` duplicates an existing binding already imported via `./runtime-api.js`, causing a `TS2300: Duplicate identifier` TypeScript error that breaks compilation of the Signal setup module.

The new null-return branch silently swallows genuine I/O errors (e.g., EACCES, EMFILE) by failing to distinguish them from benign missing-file cases, causing misconfigured or unreadable plugin entries to disappear at startup with no diagnostic emitted.

Returning `true` for grammY "Network request failed after N attempts" errors in `isSafeToRetrySendError` incorrectly marks ambiguous transport failures as safe to retry, risking duplicate message delivery for non-idempotent Telegram sends.

In `flushPendingMessages`, retrying all items in `toSend` after a mid-batch failure causes duplicate messages because `sendMSTeamsMessages` sends sequentially and earlier blocks may have already been successfully delivered before the error occurred.

Mutating a nested property of the `console` `@state` object instead of reassigning the top-level field bypasses Lit's reactivity system, causing the detail panel to fail to open or close reliably when a subagent is clicked.

The newly added `agentId` field is populated in `describeAccount()` but never reaches the primary channel-status API because `buildChannelAccountSnapshot()` only copies `projectSafeChannelAccountSnapshotFields` and does not merge `describeAccount()` output, leaving the main status surfaces without the field despite the commit's intent.

The `needsTranscriptUsageFallback` check in prewarm omits the `subagentRun?.model` fallback that `buildGatewaySessionRow` passes to `resolveSessionModelIdentityRef`, causing prewarm to incorrectly skip usage warming for child sessions with no persisted model/cost and forcing the first post-startup `sessions.list` call to perform expensive on-demand transcript reads instead.

Using `void emitUserTranscriptUpdate()` instead of awaiting it allows the assistant's transcript update to arrive before the user's in session-history subscribers, causing the assistant reply to appear before the user's prompt when image attachments are involved.

When `deliverOutboundPayloads` is called with only a `replyToId` and no `quoteAuthor`, Signal group messages silently drop native reply/quote behavior because the Signal send layer requires both fields to be present to attach quote metadata.

The `handleFeedbackInvoke` function uses the raw `activity.conversation.id` without stripping the `;messageid=...` suffix that Teams can append, causing feedback events to be routed to the wrong session when the suffixed form is present, unlike the message handler which normalizes this ID correctly.

The `gen_ai.prompt` field is recorded using `followupRun.prompt` instead of the actual `commandBody` passed to the agent, meaning the logged prompt may omit context the model truly saw (e.g., thread history, system events, or media) and include text it did not, resulting in a misleading audit trail.
Awaiting the A2A hook chain without a timeout means a single hanging plugin handler can indefinitely block the announce phase, causing users to never receive the final A2A conclusion.

A missing `executeLocal` dispatch check causes slash commands like `/clear` and `/model` to be sent as literal chat text instead of being handled locally, breaking history reset and session patching functionality.

The new snapshot path directly indexes the session store with `mainSessionKey` instead of using the `resolveSessionStoreEntry()` helper, which handles legacy/mixed-case keys, causing `mainSessionEntry` to return undefined on older installs and losing real session-specific mode and last-capture state in the Overview Cortex summary.

The new `resolveBot` function in `createHookRunner` fails to populate `bot_name` metadata for agent, session, and tool hooks because their call sites omit `accountId` from context, making the fix only effective for message hooks where account scope was already available.

The exported `gen_ai.prompt` telemetry field uses `commandBody` (the queue payload) instead of the actual prompt sent to the model, causing it to potentially include content the model never saw and omit content it did see when `diagnostics.otel.includeContent` is enabled.

The incomplete-turn fallback incorrectly synthesizes an error message ("⚠️ Agent couldn't generate a response") for same-target message sends that already succeeded, causing embedded callers to surface a visible failure even though the real reply was already delivered to the user.

When `includeMms=true`, `querySmsMmsMessagesByPhone` loads the entire conversation history into memory before applying pagination, potentially materializing thousands of rows for a request that only needs a small number of results, unlike the SMS-only path which correctly pushes `LIMIT/OFFSET` down to the content provider.

During prewarm, reading full transcript files concurrently into memory with `fs.promises.readFile` can multiply peak memory usage across up to 64 parallel tasks, risking memory pressure or OOM crashes on large deployments.

The cron path correctly skips persisting the fallback runtime model/provider, but still unconditionally writes the fallback `cliSessionId`, which can pin future cron turns to the fallback CLI session and prevent the primary model from being retried.

The new range validation (1000–120000 ms) silently clamps previously valid `OPENCLAW_HANDSHAKE_TIMEOUT_MS` environment variable values to the 15s default, breaking existing deployments that relied on out-of-range values without any warning or error.

The superseded-preview callback archives the original rendered HTML text instead of the effective text actually sent to Telegram, causing stale snapshots to corrupt regressive-update checks and finalization logic in the `forceNewMessage()` race path.

The patch inconsistently applies the normalized `effectiveStatus` to the one-shot job path but leaves the recurring-job backoff logic (lines 420–447) still checking the raw `result.status`, causing recurring jobs to be incorrectly delayed by error backoff even when the run should be treated as successful.
